issue_body:
Hello Folks,

I was thinking about the possibility of the support for some general serializers like MarshMallow and DjangoRestFramework. 

It Would Be something like:

```
from mixer.backends.drfserializer import mixer
from app.serializers import ProjectSerializer

project = mixer.blend(ProjectSerializer)
```

The biggest difference here is that we don't need to write the results to a database, just verify if the data generated is valid (call a is_valid method most the time) .

What do you think? Is this an acceptable request?

PS: I'm willing to fork and help with django rest frameworks if this request is marked as an acceptable enhancement.

issue_title: Kernel shutdown errors when a worker thread is open
issue_body:
```
Exception in thread Thread-2:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/media/storage/eyes/virtualenv/curio_dev/lib/python3.5/site-packages/curio/workers.py", line 186, in run_worker
    future.set_result(result)
  File "/media/storage/eyes/virtualenv/curio_dev/lib/python3.5/site-packages/curio/workers.py", line 132, in set_result
    self._callback(self)
  File "/media/storage/eyes/virtualenv/curio_dev/lib/python3.5/site-packages/curio/kernel.py", line 551, in <lambda>
    future.add_done_callback(lambda fut, task=current: _wake(task, fut))
  File "/media/storage/eyes/virtualenv/curio_dev/lib/python3.5/site-packages/curio/kernel.py", line 196, in _wake
    self._notify_sock.send(b'\x00')
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'send'

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/threading.py", line 914, in _bootstrap_inner
    self.run()
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/threading.py", line 862, in run
    self._target(*self._args, **self._kwargs)
  File "/media/storage/eyes/virtualenv/curio_dev/lib/python3.5/site-packages/curio/workers.py", line 188, in run_worker
    future.set_exception(e)
  File "/media/storage/eyes/virtualenv/curio_dev/lib/python3.5/site-packages/curio/workers.py", line 136, in set_exception
    self._callback(self)
  File "/media/storage/eyes/virtualenv/curio_dev/lib/python3.5/site-packages/curio/kernel.py", line 551, in <lambda>
    future.add_done_callback(lambda fut, task=current: _wake(task, fut))
  File "/media/storage/eyes/virtualenv/curio_dev/lib/python3.5/site-packages/curio/kernel.py", line 196, in _wake
    self._notify_sock.send(b'\x00')
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'send'
```

Example:

```
import queue
import threading
import curio
import time

q = queue.Queue()

def _some_thread():
    time.sleep(5)

t = threading.Thread(target=_some_thread)
t.start()
task = None

async def _worker():
    try:
        await curio.abide(q.get)
    except curio.CancelledError:
        q.put(1)  # this is crutial!

async def _main():
    global task
    task = await curio.spawn(_worker(), daemon=True)
    await task.join()

kernel = curio.Kernel()
try:
    kernel.run(_main())
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    kernel.run(curio.spawn(task.cancel()), shutdown=True)
```
Note:
  - The error *only* happens with another thread running in the background.
  - The error *only* happens when I catch the CancelledError and put an item on the queue.

This suggests that putting an item on the queue again when cancelled causes the worker thread to wake up, but because the kernel is shutting down the notify socket is dead.

The other thread being there keeps the interpreter alive, meaning the thread can still wake up to spew the error.

issue_title: Opening connection to IBM TDS crashes
issue_body:
When opening a connection to an IBM TDS your code crashes with:
...
File "/.../site-packages/ldap3/protocol/rfc4512.py", line 80, in quoted_string_to_list
    return string
IndexError: string index out of range

The problem is, that the quoted_string_to_list function is called with an empty string.
Adding something like
````python
    if len(string) == 0:
        return string
````
before accessing string[0] fixes the problem.

issue_title: Travis builds fail due to missing python2.6 and python3.3
issue_body:
@jpadilla, this is probably your call in terms of what lengths you want to go to in order to support these platforms, since it doesn't look like Travis py3.6 workers have these pythons anymore.

This happened in builds for #296, and it will impact other changes too.

### Drop 2.6?

If there's no testing on py2.6, pyjwt should drop the classifier for it: otherwise something like `"{}".format("foo")` will slip in, and on 2.6 it will break.
2.6 is _really old_ at this point. Is support for it important?

The last non-secuirty-fix release of 2.6 was almost a decade ago.

### Drop 3.3?

Python3 moves fast, and 3.3 is one of the older versions at this point.
It was released in 2012 and has been in security-fix-only maintenance since 2014 ([py3.3.7 release notes](https://www.python.org/downloads/release/python-337/)).

Unlike the issues with 2.6, if it works on 2.7 and 3.4, it _probably_ works on 3.3.
Still, IMO pyjwt should drop the classifier if it's not being tested.

### Work hard to test 2.6 and/or 3.3?

The other option seems to be to have Travis explicitly install these pythons for tests to run.
For the reasons noted above, I don't think this is worth the work involved.

If you wanted to do it, one of the better routes is probably to refactor the way tests are run to leverage Travis' python version selection, rather than trying to run everything through tox.

```python
def tabInterpConv(data, conv):  # 1 Tabular with interpolation
    """ apply Tabular interpolation conversion to data
 
    Parameters
    ----------------
    data : numpy 1D array
        raw data to be converted to physical value
    conv : mdfinfo3.info3 conversion block ('CCBlock') dict

    _K = sorted(conv.keys())

    if (all(diff(_x)) > 0):
        _y = [conv[_k]['phys'] for _k in _K]
        return interp(data, _x, _y)
    else:
        print(('X values for interpolation of channel are not increasing'))
        return data
 ```

Emmanuel

issue_title: Jedi not autocompleting when *args or **kwargs are present in function definition
issue_body:
### Summary

In Spyder 2.3.4, Jedi autocompletion does not work when `*args` or `**kwargs` are present in a function definition.

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Open up a new python file.
2. Type the following:

    ```python
    def function(*args):
        my_variable = 5
        my_v
    #      ^ Press `tab` here
    ```

3. After typing `my_v`, press <kbd>Tab</kbd>. Notice that no autocompletion happens
4. Replace `*args` with `**dict_of_args`.
5. Press <kbd>Tab</kbd> in the same location as before. Again, autocompletion does not work.
6. Remove `**dict_of_args` and repeat. Autocompletion now works.

### Expected Output

Autocompletion of variable names should work even if `*args` or `**kwargs` are present in function definition.

### Additional Info

Cross-posted in [Spyderlib Issue #2439](https://github.com/spyder-ide/spyder/issues/2439)

Version: jedi 0.9.0

issue_title: What is this python magic that I see?
issue_body:
https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/blob/1863a1c6636f53592519320a173ec9573c090c0b/discord/ext/commands/core.py#L682-L693

Can the above be simplified by just relying on `**attrs`?  Anything `**attrs` should be passed to the `cls` constructor.  It would be nice to get rid of this magical code.  I stumbled across it today, because I was trying to wrap a decorator around the `command` decorator in order to support decorating `class` methods.  All I want to do is pass `self` :man_shrugging:, but then I found out that I cannot pass `checks` in `**attrs` because it would be specified twice.

Note: `checks` could still be reversed by updating it in `**attrs`:
```python
checks = attrs.get('checks', [])
checks.reverse()
attrs['checks'] = checks
```

issue_title: lock the coverage file before accessing?
issue_body: @nedbat Maybe, lock the coverage data file with something like https://pypi.org/project/filelock/ before reading/writing it?   When using pytest-cov on Travis-CI with --cov-append, several jobs from the job matrix might be using the file concurrently.

#### Description

This new plugin will display a human reading message with a synthesis of current stats issues. For exemple if the user CPU consumption is high, the following message will be displayed:

> The processor is spending 75% of its time running user space processes. Top user process is Firefox.

### Pending questions

- Maximum size of the message ?
- Where this new plugin should be displayed in the UI ? 

### Sources

Some sources of inspiration to define human reading messages:
- For LOAD: https://injustfiveminutes.com/2013/02/28/understanding-linux-cpu-load/
- For LOAD: http://www.howtogeek.com/194642/understanding-the-load-average-on-linux-and-other-unix-like-systems/
- For LOAD: http://blog.scoutapp.com/articles/2009/07/31/understanding-load-averages
- for CPU: http://blog.scoutapp.com/articles/2015/02/24/understanding-linuxs-cpu-stats
- for MEM: http://www.infoworld.com/article/2617623/linux/making-sense-of-memory-usage-on-linux.html
- for SWAP: http://www.cyberciti.biz/faq/linux-check-swap-usage-command/

Hi Renato, 

The specification of ARFF has recently changed to allow a wider range of attribute names. More specifically, attributes are now allowed to start with numbers (in fact any character except control characters and '{', '}', ',', or '%': http://weka.wikispaces.com/ARFF+%28stable+version%29

It would be great if liac-arff could be updated to reflect this. We are using liac-arff for python clients for OpenML.org and some of our datasets (those that have attributes starting with numbers) cannot be parsed by liac-arff because of this reason.

Many thanks!

Sincerely,
Joaquin

issue_title: Removal of `class` Parameters for Non-embedded Executables
issue_body: When the first argument is an instance of a user-derived type in a subroutine/function, we remove it because fortpy assumes that the subroutine is _embedded_ in the type; but that is not always the case. We need to explicitly check the executables list for the type and make sure it is embedded before removing that argument.

issue_title: selinux module install check refers to binary not in user path
issue_body:
The selinux module tries to check whether selinux is installed.

`if not self.py3.check_commands(['getenforce']):`

this effectively runs `which getenforce`. The getenforce binary is installed under /usr/sbin (gentoo, also fedora as far as i read) which is not in the regular user path and therefore not found, resulting in the message `selinux: isn't installed`.
Removing the check makes the module work as expected.

issue_title: Issue with calculating expected status length
issue_body:
I'm not sure if this should be considered a bug or if this is the intended functionality, so I figured I'd open a ticket to see what the thoughts are...

Currently the Twitter docs at https://dev.twitter.com/basics/tco state: 

> If you are not using the opt-in features, only links shorter in length than a t.co URL will be wrapped by t.co. All links t.co-length or longer should be considered as t.co’s maximum length. For example, if help/configuration reports 20 characters as the maximum length, and a user posts a link that is 125 characters long, it should be considered as 20 characters long instead. If they post a link that is 18 characters long, it’s still only 18 characters long.

> If you are using the opt-in features, all URLs regardless of length should be considered to be the maximum length reported to you by help/configuration.

How do we determine if we're using the opt-in features?  Presently it would appear that my tweets with shortened urls (bit.ly) are being POSTed at 21 characters, and not being wrapped at all by t.co.  If this is the correct functionality, can we modify the ```calc_expected_status_length``` function to be something like the following?

```
def calc_expected_status_length(status, short_url_length=23):
    status_length = 0
    for word in re.split(r'\s', status):
        if is_url(word):
            status_length += short_url_length if len(word) >= short_url_length else len(word)
        else:
            status_length += len(word)
    status_length += len(re.findall(r'\s', status))
    return status_length
```

issue_title: Unable to declare class_registry, or sqlalchemy library incompatibility?
issue_body:
Awesome library! Thank you so much for the work that you've done here.

I can't figure out how to get sqlalchemy_continuum and sqlservice to play nicely together.

Here is some example code to reproduce the problem:

```
# model.py
# Assumes requirements met:
# SQLAlchemy-Continuum==1.3.1
# sqlservice==0.13.0

from sqlalchemy import (Column, ForeignKey, func, types)
from sqlalchemy.orm import relationship, configure_mappers
from sqlservice import ModelBase, SQLClient, declarative_base


# To use continuum, call make_versioned before declaring models
from sqlalchemy_continuum.plugins import TransactionMetaPlugin
from sqlalchemy_continuum import make_versioned
meta_plugin = TransactionMetaPlugin()
make_versioned(user_cls=None, plugins=[meta_plugin])

class_registry = {}


@declarative_base  # Possible solution:  @declarative_base(class_registry=class_registry)
class Model(ModelBase):
    pass


class User(Model):
    __versioned__ = {}
    __tablename__ = 'user'

    id = Column(types.Integer, primary_key=True)
    name = Column(types.Unicode(30))
    things = relationship('Thing', backref='user', cascade='all, delete-orphan')


class Thing(Model):
    __versioned__ = {}
    __tablename__ = 'thing'

    id = Column(types.Integer, primary_key=True)
    description = Column(types.Unicode(50))
    user_id = Column(
        types.Integer, ForeignKey('user.id', onupdate="CASCADE", ondelete="CASCADE")
    )

db = SQLClient({'SQL_DATABASE_URI': 'sqlite://'}, model_class=Model)

configure_mappers()
```

When executing this code, you should see:
```
$ python model.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "model.py", line 50, in <module>
    configure_mappers()
  File "/Users/ddp/.pyenv/versions/continuum_sqlservice/lib/python3.5/site-packages/sqlalchemy/orm/mapper.py", line 2887, in configure_mappers
    Mapper.dispatch._for_class(Mapper).after_configured()
  File "/Users/ddp/.pyenv/versions/continuum_sqlservice/lib/python3.5/site-packages/sqlalchemy/event/attr.py", line 218, in __call__
    fn(*args, **kw)
  File "/Users/ddp/.pyenv/versions/continuum_sqlservice/lib/python3.5/site-packages/sqlalchemy/orm/events.py", line 616, in wrap
    fn(*arg, **kw)
  File "/Users/ddp/.pyenv/versions/continuum_sqlservice/lib/python3.5/site-packages/sqlalchemy_continuum/builder.py", line 161, in configure_versioned_classes
    self.build_transaction_class()
  File "/Users/ddp/.pyenv/versions/continuum_sqlservice/lib/python3.5/site-packages/sqlalchemy_continuum/builder.py", line 141, in build_transaction_class
    self.manager.create_transaction_model()
  File "/Users/ddp/.pyenv/versions/continuum_sqlservice/lib/python3.5/site-packages/sqlalchemy_continuum/manager.py", line 158, in create_transaction_model
    self.transaction_cls = self.transaction_cls(self)
  File "/Users/ddp/.pyenv/versions/continuum_sqlservice/lib/python3.5/site-packages/sqlalchemy_continuum/factory.py", line 9, in __call__
    registry = manager.declarative_base._decl_class_registry
AttributeError: type object 'ModelBase' has no attribute '_decl_class_registry'
```

It is likely that this "problem" is not really an issue and is in fact due to my inexperience with SQLAlchemy in general, but I see that SQLAlchemy's declarative_base function accepts a class_registry parameter. I shall follow up this issue with a pull request containing a potential fix, but if I have misdiagnosed the problem and you see another solution that involves configuration or subclassing rather than a code change, I would prefer that to imposing change on your library.

issue_title: Slow shrinking gives poor dev experience
issue_body:
Hi,

I've been trying out hypothesis today, and found a rough edge.

I have a strategy for producing a recursive data structure. I am
using it in a test. I am writing this while waiting for the test to
return, so far it has been 10 minutes.

If I set max_shrinks=0 then run again I get a failure near-instantly.

I suspect that it is having trouble shrinking the example.
(My uneducated guess is that the *structure* matters a lot
for the test, but all the internal details don't, so it is trying
a lot of examples that tinker with the internal values that
have no bearing on the test.)

I've read that hypothesis has a default timeout of 60s,
but that doesn't seem to apply to shrinking.

Overall while it's great that it is finding interesting cases
for me, the fact that it isn't showing them to me isn't helpful.
I'd prefer a larger example now to a smaller one in 10 minutes.

Some possible ideas:

  * apply the timeout the shrinking stage too.
  * reduce the default max_shrinks.
  * when an example is found show that to the user, and then attempt to shrink it. They can puzzle on the unshrunk version while it works.

Thanks for working on this.

James

issue_title: Shared Parameters of Combined Light / Mass Profiles
issue_body:
Lets say we are modeling a lens galaxy's light profile using a double Sersic profile:

sersic1 = EllipticalSersic(...)
sersic2 = EllipticalSersic(...)
light_profile = CombinedLightProfile(sersic1, sersic2)

If the two light profiles are completely independent, this means each is controlled by 7 nonlinear parameters, giving us a total of 14 non-linear parameters.

In many modeling situations, it is unclear if the two Sersic's should share the same geometry. For example, we might want the second Sersic to share the same centre as the first sersic. This must be reflected in the priors we give sersic2, as it no longer draws its values of the centre from a prior but simply acquires the values from sersic1. The same could happen for the rotation angle and ellipticity. Thus, we need the code to be able to know if the centers of two light profiles are shared, that there are now just 12 non-linear parameters.

This could feasibly happen when there are 3 Light profiles (e.g. a Sersic + Sersic + Sersic) and we need flexibility in choosing if sersic3 and sersic2 are aligned with sersic1, or not.

This also occurs for mass profiles. For example, we may have a dark matter halo given by an EllipticalNFWProfile, and may want to align it with the centre or rotation angle of our CombinedLightProfile (somehow picking which component we align it with). Thus, for any mass profile, we need the option too tie its geometry to given component of a CombinedLightProfile.

Mass profiles also have some level of shared parameters. For example, a SersicMassProfile has a mass to light ratio. If a CombinedMassProfile is formed of two SersicMassProfiles, the question arises whether they share the same mass_to_light ratio, or use indepedent values.

This a place to discuss units used to be used when processing and returning performance data. 

The basic question is  1KB = 1000B or 1KB = 1024B? The Nagios Ecosystem does not make this clear. 

According to wikipedia the '1000' base should be used with KB
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilobyte

But looking at the actual code for the 'offical' nagios plug-ins they appear to consistantly use a base of '1024'. Best examples are `check_disk` and `check_swap`
https://github.com/nagios-plugins/nagios-plugins/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=1024&type=

To add more fuel to the fire I see code in pnp4nagios that looks to use base 1000.

I am inclined to go for 1024 as that seems to be what the Nagios folks intended the meaning to be but I would love to hear other points of view.

issue_title: checking via zest-releaser fails if setuptools is provided by a buildout
issue_body:
my use-case, I have a "tools"-buildout that installs zest.releaser, fabric, and check-manifest,...
this buildout is bootstrapped from a clean python that deliberatetly has no setuptools installed, 
(so it can never conflict with a buildout-provided setuptools).

the check_manifest takes sys.executable as default python
see https://github.com/mgedmin/check-manifest/blob/master/check_manifest.py#L572
while this is correct, the executable looses its sys.path, 

A similar issue was fixed in zest.releaser, see
https://github.com/zestsoftware/zest.releaser/issues/57
https://github.com/zestsoftware/zest.releaser/pull/58/files

as far as I see, the same solution ban be re-used.

Now that we know how to solve PyPy problems, time to look at PyPy3 ;-). Not that it's very important given that it has a builtin lzma module but still we could fix it for completeness. The error is:

```
cc -pthread -fPIC -I/usr/lib64/pypy3/include -I/home/mgorny/include -I/opt/local/include -I/usr/local/include -I/usr/lib64/pypy3/include -c backports/lzma/_lzmamodule.c -o build/temp.linux-x86_64-3.5/backports/lzma/_lzmamodule.o
backports/lzma/_lzmamodule.c: In function ‘_PyUnicode_FromId’:
backports/lzma/_lzmamodule.c:87:18: warning: implicit declaration of function ‘PyUnicode_DecodeUTF8Stateful’ [-Wimplicit-function-declaration]
     id->object = PyUnicode_DecodeUTF8Stateful(id->string,
                  ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
backports/lzma/_lzmamodule.c:87:16: warning: assignment makes pointer from integer without a cast [-Wint-conversion]
     id->object = PyUnicode_DecodeUTF8Stateful(id->string,
                ^
```

My initial guess is that the `PyUnicode_DecodeUTF8Stateful` is an internal CPython function that extensions are not supposed to use. [Documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/unicode.html#utf-8-codecs) does not include that function, and [random stackoverflow post](https://stackoverflow.com/a/36099157/165333) seems to confirm that.

issue_title: Confusion Matrix plots are confusing with long categories
issue_body:
When you create a confusion matrix plot for a classification problem that has a lot of categories with long names, the names for the "Predicted label" axis can overlap, causing the axis to become unreadable. Even using large dimensions for the `figsize` parameter isn't enough in some cases.

Here is an example.

![scikit_plot_01](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4276196/27996318-96ff65e0-64ad-11e7-8885-50f9fb79683c.png)

Perhaps there could be a new optional parameter added to allow the labels on the "Predicted label" axis to be rotated 90 degrees in order to make them easier to read.

issue_title: Modal dialog min-width issue: cutoff in thinner browser windows
issue_body: My experience so far with this extension has mostly been pretty great! However, one issue in particular has been bugging me. Whenever any modal shows up - be it the rename modal, the deletion modal, or something else - it is cutoff when my browser windows are approximately half-screen width. It looks fine in full-screen mode. I don't have this issue with the default theme, where it looks like the modal width is percentage-based. I assume that's the fix. Looking at the page in Chrome's dev-tools, I see that the `modal-dialog` class appears to govern these modals. Its `min-width` is set to 950px. Removing that property or setting it to a percentage width appears to fix the problem. Here is an image illustrating the issue, comparing the default theme with my dark theme setup at half-screen width: My settings for `jupyter-themes` are as described in the screenshot, namely `jt -t onedork -altout -cellw 88% -vim`. No combination of existing options I'm aware of affects modal width at all.

issue_title: Can cached_property be a subclass of property?
issue_body:
There are certain pieces of code in some libraries, linters and static analyzers that check things like

```python
if isinstance(attr, property):
    do_something()
```

This is especially important for use in metaclasses.

Obviously this fails with cached property; would it be possible to derive it from `property` so isinstance/issubclass checks work as expected?

``` >>> from markdown import markdown >>> a=''' ...``` ... test bash ... this is a test script! ...``` ... ''' >>> >>> markdown(a) u'

test bash\nthis is a test script!

' >>> ``` it is \\, not \\. how?

[Original Issue](https://github.com/slazarov/python-bittrex-websocket/issues/12)
Wanna check if there is problem on other websites.

When I want to run the test suite from within a package build of jaraco.collections I need to have the dependencies of the packages installed.  So I list jaraco.text as a BuildRequires.  This installs the distro package of jaraco.text (good!) but since that requires jaraco.collections, the distro package of jaraco.collections is also installed.

Then when I run the tests via pytest, pytest finds both the distro package and the local package and emits that ImportMismatchError.

I'm not sure why pytest is finding both versions -- I've tried setting PYTHONPATH to the directory I'm building jaraco.collections in to try to have the local version picked up first but that has no effect.  I have found an ugly, ugly workaround which I'm using to build and test the package for now: in the toplevel directory,
```
mv jaraco/ j/
python2 setup.py pytest
mv j/ jaraco/
```

I'd love to get rid of that hack but I'm not sure what py.test is really complaining about or how to really fix it.

issue_title: sftp.SFTPStorageFile doesn't properly call it's parent __init__
issue_body:
Django's file object assumes it has a `.name` attribute, like in the `__bool__` method here: https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.11.12/django/core/files/base.py#L31-L32

It does so, because it sets this attribute in its constructor here: https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.11.12/django/core/files/base.py#L19-L21

However, the `sftp.SFTPStorageFile` object does not call that constructor:  https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.11.12/django/core/files/base.py#L19-L21

Simply adding in `super(SFTPStorageFile, self).__init__(file=self.file, name=self._name)` at the end of that method solves the issue. I'm not setup to make a PR at the moment, or this would also include a PR. I'm happy to make one later today.

issue_title: end's timezone is not normalized in Event._get_occurrenece_list
issue_body: In `Event._get_occurrenece_list`, the rrule's `dtstart` is normalized to `start`'s timezone, but the `end` parameter's is not. This can lead to wrong results if `end` (or the event's `end_recurring_period` if it's smaller) is in a different timezone.

[1] http://docs.python-requests.org/en/latest/user/advanced/#ca-certificates

issue_body:

Thanks !

I ran into an interoperability issue caused by error handling in restplus that has surfaced in the flask-jwt-extended repo (vimalloc/flask-jwt-extended#86, vimalloc/flask-jwt-extended#83) and did a little digging to figure out where things are going awry and found a few different problems. To show my work, I've created a gist with server code and three logs for specific requests (both client and server side): https://gist.github.com/abathur/5d9de7c2adbbd3f2c85e50e5f81fb267

I'm not really sure what the "solution" is (and FWIW, I suspect multiple open flask-restplus issues regarding exceptions/debugging are related to these interop issues), but hopefully this will help.

## Problem 1: Flask/app-registered user error handlers aren't getting called on restplus-managed endpoints.

- When we have used `@app.errorhandler(ExceptionClass)` to specify our own error handler, `app.handle_user_exception` is responsible for calling the decorated function in order for it to construct a response, but flask_restplus is injecting `api.error_router` in the way and intercepting all exceptions.
- `api.error_router` will pass exceptions for non-restplus endpoints back to the original `app.handle_user_exception`, but _all_ exceptions for restplus endpoints get passed to `api.handle_error` and only get passed back in the event of an exception in `api.handle_error`. I think this would be fine if `api.handle_error` intentionally re-raised non-restplus exceptions.
- If a given exception hasn't been handled before the "else" in the first if-else block of `api.handle_error`, it will get assigned an `INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR` code and message.

## Problem 2: Restplus is eating exceptions that occur within the try-except in `api.error_router` (instead returning cryptic/misleading assertion errors from Flask), but at least some such exceptions can result from problems in user code. I'm guessing many developers could sort out issues mentioning these assertion errors before reporting if the relevant error wasn't getting swallowed.

These errors may look like:
```
...
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/flask/app.py", line 1542, in handle_exception
    raise e
AssertionError
```
or:
```
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/flask/app.py", line 1504, in handle_user_exception
    assert exc_value is e
AssertionError
```

Not sure how widespread these may be, but one simple example I found is registering an incorrect error-handler with restplus which fails to return a flask response.

## Problem 3: The restplus @errorhandler API appears to just do a class equality check (in `handle_error()`), whereas the equivalent API in Flask (see `flask/flask/app.py::_find_error_handler()`) checks the full MRO for the exception to see if the user has registered a handler for any of the parent classes. I haven't gone looking for examples, but I would guess some users of this feature expect equivalent behavior.

issue_title: Context (inc request) should be passed to serializer in introspection
issue_body:
I use `kwargs['context']['request']` in my serializer `__init__`'s in order to restrict querysets based on the users access privileges and remove some superuser-only fields.

This fails with django-rest-swagger since no context is passed in:

https://github.com/marcgibbons/django-rest-swagger/blob/0.3.4/rest_framework_swagger/introspectors.py#L424

Could/should a context with a fake request (inc user) be passed into serializers similarly to how it is done in views?

https://github.com/marcgibbons/django-rest-swagger/blob/0.3.4/rest_framework_swagger/introspectors.py#L226-L228

issue_title: API error "Could not acquire lock on fulfillment" handled incorrectly
issue_body:

Response(code=422, body="{"errors":"Could not acquire lock on fulfillment."}", headers=...)

This seems very much parallel to

and should probably be fixed. Right now it will raise a KeyError and the user will be completely in the dark as to what went wrong.

Best,
Tobias

issue_title: GoogleNet only has one loss output?
issue_body: The standard GoogleNet tries to minimize three softmax losses, while the GoogleNet in MXNet (as defined in [the symble file](https://github.com/dmlc/mxnet/blob/master/example/image-classification/symbol_googlenet.py)) only has one softmax loss at the end. Is there any reason for that? Is it simply because one softmax loss leads to almost the same performance compared to the standard?

issue_title: Would it be a good idea to output initialisation details?
issue_body: I found that the initialisation has no rich information, so it is hard to make sure if the initialisation is correct. Would it be better to output the everything done in initialisation step like what weights are initialised with what values? Like caffe, it has different level of log, so we can see the info we need.

issue_title: Sample Data or example application showing multiple spans
issue_body:
Hi,

Is there example traces or sample data I can download to get an idea of how to use the attributes in zipkin? I wanted to trace how a particular event is processed through our components. Those events have unique identifiers and I am wondering how I should be passing them in to zipkin. I am going to be using python and Go instrumentation for our components. Just wondering about best practices etc.

I have been tracing for some time now and I can't seem to see multiple spans with this lib. I just see one span for my services. I even set cutom span and parent_span ID's to be passed in.

Some sample code:
A task being queued 
```
        zattr = ZipkinAttrs(trace_id=self.message.id.replace('-',''), span_id=self.message.id.replace('-',''), parent_span_id=None, flags=None, is_sampled=False)
        with zipkin_span(
            service_name='trace',
            span_name="stage2:consumer",
            zipkin_attrs=zattr,
            transport_handler=transport_handler,
            sample_rate=0.1,
            port=42,
        ):
            self._task.delay(self.message)
```
A worker executing the task
```
        if method is not None:
            zattr = ZipkinAttrs(trace_id=self.message.id.replace('-',''), span_id=generate_random_64bit_string(), parent_span_id=self.message.id.replace('-',''), flags=None, is_sampled=False)
            with zipkin_span(
                service_name='trace',
                span_name="stage3:worker",
                zipkin_attrs=zattr,
                transport_handler=transport_handler,
                sample_rate=0.1,
                port=42,
            ):
                method()
```

I should be seeing a root trace and 2 spans right? or have I misunderstood something fundamental?

Thanks,

Currently, adding `null=True` to a `JSONField` is accepted, but silently coerces to `'{}'`. This behaviour is both unexpected, and can impact the performance of migrations (which might be performed during a site release, therefore causing downtime).

As a user, I would expect that if nullable JSON fields are not supported, that an exception would be raised when instantiating with `null=True`.

If nullable fields are supposed to be supported, I would expect to be able to filter querysets with `__isnull=True`, and I would expect that passing `null=True` would result in a null field being added to the table in the database. In the case of Postgres, null fields are "free" to add to a table, so this is desirable with large tables.

I really have no idea what's happening here:

```
$ ./manage.py shell_plus                                                                                                                                                                                                                       
# Shell Plus Model Imports
from allauth.account.models import EmailAddress, EmailConfirmation
from allauth.socialaccount.models import SocialAccount, SocialApp, SocialToken
...
# Shell Plus Django Imports
from django.utils import timezone
from django.conf import settings
from django.core.cache import cache
from django.db.models import Avg, Count, F, Max, Min, Sum, Q
from django.core.urlresolvers import reverse
from django.db import transaction
Python 2.7.6 (default, Jun 22 2015, 17:58:13) 
Type "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.

IPython 3.2.1 -- An enhanced Interactive Python.
?         -> Introduction and overview of IPython's features.
%quickref -> Quick reference.
help      -> Python's own help system.
object?   -> Details about 'object', use 'object??' for extra details.

In [1]:   
```

At this point, the cursor is where we expect, but it doesn't accept input. Anything I type (including ctrl-c) just gets queued, and when I finally kill the process then all my typing arrives at the bash prompt.

If I use `./manage.py shell_plus --ipython` (instead of the default ptpython) then it works fine. Also if I run `ptpython` at the command-line, that works fine too.

issue_title: feature request - configurable options for HTMLSerializer
issue_body:
I ran into a weird issue.  It would seem that running something through the bleach multiple times will result in different output.  here's a testcase - https://gist.github.com/jvanasco/2cda35740de8afc0a003

what happens is that the underlying HTMLSerializer will build the serialize the text differently, depending on the output

pay attention to the order of the attributes here:

    <button class="navbar-toggle collapsed" data-target=".navbar-collapse" data-toggle="collapse" type="button">

and here:

    <button data-target=".navbar-collapse" class="navbar-toggle collapsed" data-toggle="collapse" type="button">

html5lib seems to order the attributes "not randomly" based on their initial ordering.  they re-order endlessly between the two .

in our case, we "bleach" user content on input.  

on certain screens, if something had to be bleached, we present the bleached result to the user to approve.  because of this behavior, bleaching the content a second time will result in a document that doesn't match the input document.  

there are workarounds that we employ for now, but it's messy.  we just patched bleach to pass `alphabetical_attributes=True` into HTMLSerializer

it would be great if bleach either had some more default options for HTMLSerialize ( in `_serialize` ) or allowed for some configuration of defaults.

Matplotlib is supporting unicode literals rather than string literals and during my traitlets refactor I've come across this issue where `traitlets/traitlets/utils/importstring.py` will throw an error because the `fromlist` argument in `__import__` doesn't accept unicode:

```python
from __future__ import unicode_literals
from traitlets import Instance, HasTraits

class Artist(HasTraits):
    axes = Instance('matplotlib.axes.Axes')

a = Artist()
```

Even though `__import__` in Py3 accepts unicode, dealing with the unicode literals in Py2 would require that every class (that needs to be imported with an import string) instead read:

```python
class Artist(HasTraits):
    axes = Instance(str('matplotlib.axes.Axes'))
```

If there isn't a way around this problem it might be worth fixing.

issue_title: Cache outputs generated by the WebUI
issue_body:
Exporting outputs via the WebUI is an expensive operation: i.e. an hmap for a national wide computation takes, on Wilson, 10 minutes.

With the current workflow the output is dumped to `/tmp` and then sent via HTTP.
Files in `/tmp` are created with a random name, so a new export is always performed even if a copy of it already exists on the filesystem. This is very inefficient from both the CPU and disk space usage.

We should generate outputs with a known name and before asking the engine to export an output we can check if the file already exists on the filesystem.

I don't see issue with cache invalidation, in the sense that if we use a combination of ID and checksum we can be sure that the output on fs is always the right one (one a calculation is complete it should be immutable).

Using the following code

```
from __future__ import print_function

import json
import boto3

s3 = boto3.client('s3')
data = s3.head_object( Bucket='mytestbucket231312', Key="test.sh" ) 
data['LastModified'] = data['LastModified'].strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S')
print( json.dumps(data) )
```
The documentation (https://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference/services/s3.html#S3.Client.head_object) says it should respond with the "StorageClass", but it does not

```
{
  "AcceptRanges": "bytes",

}
```

Python library not compatible with HMAC, Base64 in Python 3.4
issue_body:
When attempting to use the library in a Python 3.4 environment, the _sign_request() function will always return ERR_UNKNOWN. This comes down to the _sign_vals() function passing str objects to the underlying functions, instead of byte objects.

Underlying errors include:
TypeError: 'str' does not support the buffer interface
TypeError: Unicode-objects must be encoded before hashing

Re-writing _sign_vals() to look like this:
def _sign_vals(key, vals, prefix, expire):
    exp = str(int(time.time()) + expire)

    val = '|'.join(vals + [ exp ])
    b64 = base64.b64encode(str.encode(val))
    cookie = '%s|%s' % (prefix, bytes.decode(b64))

    sig = _hmac_sha1(str.encode(key), str.encode(cookie))
    return '%s|%s' % (cookie, sig)

works just fine in Python 3.4, and in manual testing of the same functions in 2.7. As soon as I can work out how to put up

Instagram released their competing tool, [MonkeyType](https://github.com/Instagram/MonkeyType) ([blog](https://engineering.instagram.com/let-your-code-type-hint-itself-introducing-open-source-monkeytype-a855c7284881), [docs](https://monkeytype.readthedocs.io/en/stable/)).

A possibly road to convergence might involve sharing type storage and configuration so you can use either tool to collect types and the other to apply them.

issue_title: _get_put_file_sources does not return folders containing square brackets
issue_body:
https://github.com/robotframework/SSHLibrary/blob/42785d9a9a1649c6006a0550ce150a30f4b78af6/src/SSHLibrary/abstractclient.py#L997
Folders that contain square brackets in their name (ie. 2018-08-16_10-31-48_TEST-[1]test_name) are not properly returned by this list comprehension. Glob.glob is the culprit here since [ ] are considered special characters and need to be escaped.
This is important because test tracking software like IBM Quality Center uses naming conventions that require every test to start with [1] and automatic test reporting to QC is in this case much easier if TC name is aligned with QC name.

issue_title: Is there a class or a function to build PSF model from the stars in the image?
issue_body:
Hi,

I am heavily using the IRAF's psf task in the daophot package to build a PSF model from the stars in an image. This gives the power to create a spatially variable PSF model that I can use with allstar. Is there a way to do the exact same thing within photutils? I understand that prepare_psf_model can be used to create the model, but as far as I understand you don't use stars to fit moffat or gauss functions, like in the IRAF's psf task. It's just not clear to me how can one do this. Is there are tutorial or example that I'm missing to find?

Thanks!
Iskren

        for upgrade_dir in [upgrade_dir_1, upgrade_dir_2]:
            with silence():
                run_setup(str(upgrade_dir.join('setup.py')),
                          ['sdist', '--dist-dir=dist', '--formats=gztar'])
            dists.append(str(upgrade_dir.join('dist')))
            for dist_file in upgrade_dir.visit('*.tar.gz'):
                dist_file.copy(source.join('dists'))
    
        # Monkey with the PackageIndex in ah_bootstrap so that it is initialized
        # with the test upgrade packages, and so that it does not actually go out
        # to the internet to look for anything
        import ah_bootstrap
    
        class FakePackageIndex(PackageIndex):
            def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
                PackageIndex.__init__(self, *args, **kwargs)
                self.to_scan = dists
    
            def find_packages(self, requirement):
                # no-op
                pass
    
        ah_bootstrap.PackageIndex = FakePackageIndex
    
        try:
            with source.as_cwd():
                # Now run the source setup.py; this test is similar to
                # test_download_if_needed, but we explicitly check that the correct
                # *version* of _astropy_helpers_test_ was used
                run_setup('setup.py', [])
    
                stdout, stderr = capsys.readouterr()
                path = stdout.splitlines()[-1].strip()
                eggs = _get_local_eggs()

I don't have time to investigate for now. Does anyone have any ideas?
For pytest plugins, it's not common to put the source inside the ``src`` directory. So the ``pytest_remotedata`` directory should be moved up to the top level.

issue_title: skimage.segmentation.active_contour is too sensitive to arguments' dtypes
issue_body:
Gallery example (works):
```
img = data.astronaut()
img = rgb2gray(img)  # float64
s = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 400)
x = 220 + 100*np.cos(s)
y = 100 + 100*np.sin(s)
init = np.array([x, y]).T  # float64
snake = active_contour(gaussian(img, 3), init)
```

Reported case:
```
img = data.astronaut()
img = rgb2gray(img)
init = np.tile(np.arange(img.shape[1]).reshape(-1, 1), (1, 2))  # int32
snake_out = active_contour(gaussian(img, 3), init)
```
returns:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "segm_comp.py", line 34, in <module>
    snake_out = segmentation.active_contour(gaussian(img, 3), init)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/site-packages/skimage/segmentation/active_contour_model.py", line 219, in active_contour
    x[:] += dx
TypeError: Cannot cast ufunc add output from dtype('float64') to dtype('int32') with casting rule 'same_kind'
```

If `init = np.tile(np.arange(img.shape[1]).reshape(-1, 1), (1, 2))` is casted with `.astype(np.float)`, everything works fine regardless to the dtype of `img`(both `int32` and `float64` work fine).

[Function signature](http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/api/skimage.segmentation.html#skimage.segmentation.active_contour) doesn't mention any dtype limitations for `snake` argument.

issue_title: Add `np.lookfor` as a discoverability tip to the User Guide
issue_body:
scikit-image's package structure is problematic for functions that span categories. For example, `watershed` segmentation is in morphology, because it is traditionally viewed as a morphological transformation. Conversely, `find_boundaries` is in segmentation, but some users have [looked for it](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/2396#issuecomment-270022280) in morphology.

I've advocated for creating a flat scikit-image namespace before (https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/1608), but that discussion stalled when `np.lookfor` was [mooted](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/1608#issuecomment-121798358) as an 80% solution.

However, that is a solution only for those who know about it. So, I propose that we add a section on API discovery to the [user guide](scikit-image.org/docs/dev/user_guide.html).

```python
import numpy as np
np.lookfor('boundaries', 'skimage')
```
or
```python
import skimage
np.lookfor('boundaries', skimage)
```

Hi, you can see a reason for removing that image on the corresponding Debian bug report [1]
do you think you can replace that image with a more "open source" one?

[1] https://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=794859

thanks!

This is inspired by some of the answers in [this SO question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/14749328/python-how-to-check-whether-optional-function-parameter-is-set).

I recently needed to specify a default value to a parameter where `None` was a possible value that people might want to pass, and further it was the default value that needed deprecating, so our usual pathway of "set the default to None and then change it to a real value in the future" didn't work. I needed to distinguish between "default None" and "user-given None".

The top answers in SO suggested the following:

```python
DEFAULT = object()

def myfunc(param=DEFAULT):
    if param is DEFAULT:
        warn(...)   # etc
    # etc
```

This works, but it'll be messy in the documentation:

```python
>>> DEFAULT=object()
>>> str(DEFAULT)
'<object object at 0x11dfc3370>'
```

This whole thing has been turning in my head since then and I think that we can actually make a Default class that (a) prints the right thing to the docs, so that function introspection works, and (b) greatly simplifies our deprecation procedures for default value changes.

Here's the proposal in brief:

```python
class Default:
    def __init__(self, value, next_value=None,
                 change_version=None, param_name=None):
        self.value = value
        self.next_value = next_value
        self.change_version = change_version
        self.param_name = param_name
        if next_value is not None:
            self.message = (f'The default value of {param_name} will '
                            f'change to {next_value} in {change_version}.')
        else:
            self.message = ''

    def __str__(self):
        if self.next_value is None:
            return str(self.value)
        else:
            return (f'{self.value} ({self.next_value} after '
                    f'{self.change_version}')

    def __repr__(self):
        return (f'Default({repr(self.value)}, {repr(self.param_name}, '
                f'{repr(self.next_value)}, {repr(self.change_version)})')

    def warn(self):
        if self.next_value is not None:
            warn(self.message)
```

Use it like this. If we want to deprecate an argument default value from False to True, change:

```python
def my_func(my_deprecated_arg=False):
```

to:

```python
def my_func(my_deprecated_arg=Default(False, True, 'v0.16',
                                      'my_deprecated_arg')):
    if type(my_deprecated_arg) == Default:
        my_deprecated_arg.warn()
```

And that's it! No docstrings to update, with default information that may become stale, no inconsistent deprecation messages to write, and the docs write themselves. In fact, with the right Regex in the release docs, we don't even need to add an item to the to-do: the release manager can just search for `Default...v0.whatever`.

@scikit-image/core, if you like this pattern, 👍 and I'll write the PR. If not, then fire up the comments and we'll see if it is salvageable. =)

issue_title: scikit-image sprint at BIDS May 28 - Jun 1
issue_body:
## Description
This issue collects ideas and issues for the scikit-image side of the joint scikit-learn/scikit-image/dask sprint at UC Berkeley. 

- work on the parallelization of some algorithms to improve performance, focussing maybe first on the case of machines with several cores (rather than a distributed architecture, which most users don't use I guess). This would need some benchmarking and we could test several solutions such as dask (with Matt Rocklin, we could test and maybe improve our apply_parallel) as well as joblib and its different backends (with Gaël and Olivier). Emma can provide some tomography datasets for this, it would be better if we had a machine with 10-20 cores to ssh on for benchmarking, maybe with AWS? Or a BIDS machine?
- discussing all [GitHub "needs decision" PRs/issues](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/labels/status%3A%20needs%20decision). 
- get nD transforms, based on NumPy array coordinates, working. (with @kne42)
- figure out whether we can reliably distribute Numba, and if so, do we want to get that dependency.
- implement flood-fill with support for lowlevelcallables (see https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/2876#issuecomment-385241507)
- fix data types and ranges. (See [#3009](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/3009).)
- discuss and implement possible matmul interface for transforms instead of add. (with @hmaarrfk)
- talk about release schedules, governance, funding, leadership, outreach, and a few more general topics. (Though @jni thinks these things should happen in the evenings over dinner etc. ;)
- deprecate the skimage viewer to move it to its own repo.

Stuff that we want to happen *before* the sprint:
- [x] announce the sprint on the mailing list to see whether anyone wants to join remotely
- [x] release 0.14
- [ ] get airspeed velocity working (though maybe this needs to happen *before* the sprint anyway)
- [ ] draw up scikit-image 1.0 roadmap (including emailing mailing list for community discussion)

Note also [this repo](https://github.com/scisprints/2018_05_sklearn_skimage_dask) for ideas for *joint* work with sklearn and dask teams. I suspect @jakirkham will be the ringleader for that. =)

The TM algorithm implementation from nupic.core preserves the algorithm but radically changes the methodology to provide performance increases but will affect testing... Therefore, the carried-over changes must be vetted rigorously and tests should reflect the same determinant results because they are used as porting tools also...

issue_title: Move TAL specification from Zope2Book to here, likewise for TALES, ZPT
issue_body:
It currently lives at https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope2docs/blob/master/docs/zope2book/AppendixC.rst and on the web at https://docs.zope.org/zope2/zope2book/AppendixC.html.

It seems to me that it would make more sense if it lived here and was published at zopetal.readthedocs.io, as the "Zope 2" book gives the wrong idea about what it is and who uses it.

I'd like to extract the TAL and  METAL parts of the spec from AppendixC and bring them to a document here, and then update AppendixC to refer to the new locations.  I'd like to do the same for the TALES and Page Template sections of that document for zope.tales and zope.pagetemplate. The TALES document in particular is missing documentation for some expression types (lazy and defer) and could use some updating.

Possible concern: zopefoundation/Zope appears to use both zope.pagetemplate/zope.tales and z3c.pt for its implementation of page templates (it's not clear to me exactly what gets used for what), so by bringing the documentation closer to this implementation we might deviate from what is actually available in Zope2. To an extent this has already happened, as `first` and `last` are not available in zope.tal/zope.tales [or Chameleon](https://github.com/malthe/chameleon/issues/249)---to the extent that I know about implementation differences, I can call them out in the documentation.

Thoughts?

I tried to build an simple test app using buildozer on ubuntu14.04 and I understood that buildozer cant seem to download and process android ndk version r10e. 

The problem is that the ndk downloads have changed recently, they are not in ".tar.bz2" format, but instead are given as ".bin" files.

I found where the problem comes from and in order to fix them, here might be a way:
- In the "buildozer/targets/android.py", the function _install_android_ndk(), has no implementation to make the ".bin" urls. So using re module, the problem could be solved, i think.

Check the "CODE BEGIN HERE" SECTIONS
```python
def _install_android_ndk(self):
        ndk_dir = self.android_ndk_dir
        if self.buildozer.file_exists(ndk_dir):
            self.buildozer.info('Android NDK found at {0}'.format(ndk_dir))
            return ndk_dir

        # CODE BEGIN HERE
        import re   # just needed to check if version is greater than 9
        _version = re.search('[a-z](.+?)[a-z]', self.android_ndk_version).group(1)
        # CODE END HERE

        self.buildozer.info('Android NDK is missing, downloading')
        if platform in ('win32', 'cygwin'):
            # Checking of 32/64 bits at Windows from: http://stackoverflow.com/a/1405971/798575
            import struct
            archive = 'android-ndk-r{0}-windows-{1}.zip'
            is_64 = (8*struct.calcsize("P") == 64)
            
        elif platform in ('darwin', ):
            # CODE BEGIN HERE
            if int(_version) > 9: # if greater than 9, take it as .bin file
                archive = 'android-ndk-r{0}-darwin-{1}.bin'
            else:
                archive = 'android-ndk-r{0}-darwin-{1}.tar.bz2'
            # CODE END HERE
            is_64 = (os.uname()[4] == 'x86_64')

        elif platform.startswith('linux'):
            # CODE BEGIN HERE
            if int(_version) > 9: # if greater than 9, take it as .bin file
                archive = 'android-ndk-r{0}-linux-{1}.bin'
            else:
                archive = 'android-ndk-r{0}-linux-{1}.tar.bz2'
            # CODE END HERE
            is_64 = (os.uname()[4] == 'x86_64')
        else:
            raise SystemError('Unsupported platform: {0}'.format(platform))

        architecture = 'x86_64' if is_64 else 'x86'
        unpacked = 'android-ndk-r{0}'
        archive = archive.format(self.android_ndk_version, architecture)
        unpacked = unpacked.format(self.android_ndk_version)
        url = 'http://dl.google.com/android/ndk/'
        self.buildozer.download(url, archive,
                cwd=self.buildozer.global_platform_dir)

        self.buildozer.info('Unpacking Android NDK')
        self.buildozer.file_extract(archive,
                cwd=self.buildozer.global_platform_dir)
        self.buildozer.file_rename(unpacked, ndk_dir,
                cwd=self.buildozer.global_platform_dir)
        self.buildozer.info('Android NDK installation done.')
        return ndk_dir
```
- In the "buildozer/__init__.py", the function file_extract() has no way to extract the ".bin" file. so we could modify it like this and it might work.

```python
def file_extract(self, archive, cwd=None):
        if archive.endswith('.tgz') or archive.endswith('.tar.gz'):
            # XXX tarfile doesn't work for NDK-r8c :(
            #tf = tarfile.open(archive, 'r:*')
            #tf.extractall(path=cwd)
            #tf.close()
            self.cmd('tar xzf {0}'.format(archive), cwd=cwd)
            return

        if archive.endswith('.tbz2') or archive.endswith('.tar.bz2'):
            # XXX same as before
            self.cmd('tar xjf {0}'.format(archive), cwd=cwd)
            return

        # CODE BEGIN HERE
        if archive.endswith('.bin'):
            # To process the bin files for linux and darwin systems
            self.cmd('chmod a+x {0}'.format(archive),cwd=cwd)
            self.cmd('./{0}'.format(archive),cwd=cwd)
            return
        # CODE END HERE

        if archive.endswith('.zip'):
            archive = join(cwd, archive)
            zf = zipfile.ZipFile(archive)
            zf.extractall(path=cwd)
            zf.close()
            return

        raise Exception('Unhandled extraction for type {0}'.format(archive))
```

So if anybody has the time, they can implement this and kinda update the code. I would love to do it, but the thing is I just dont know how to do that(never worked on multiple contributor based codes).

issue_title: Would it be possbile to include the .dlls in the binary download?
issue_body:
Hi,

would it be possible to include the .dll files in the binary downloads for Windows?
The .dlls are kind of needed to make the JNI wrappers work. This is especially a problem for the encoder as there is no Java implementation (which is a huge adoption problem IMHO).

I already tried to build the .dlls myself with CMake and MinGW, but I was not able to make the JNI encoder wrapper work with them. I always got an UnsatisfiedLinkError on instantiation with libbrotlicommon.dll and libbrotlienc.dll loaded.

>java.lang.UnsatisfiedLinkError: org.brotli.wrapper.enc.EncoderJNI.nativeCreate([J)Ljava/nio/ByteBuffer;
        at org.brotli.wrapper.enc.EncoderJNI.nativeCreate(Native Method)
        at org.brotli.wrapper.enc.EncoderJNI.access$000(EncoderJNI.java:15)
        at org.brotli.wrapper.enc.EncoderJNI$Wrapper.<init>(EncoderJNI.java:36)
        at org.brotli.wrapper.enc.Encoder.compress(Encoder.java:159)
        at org.brotli.wrapper.enc.Encoder.compress(Encoder.java:196)

I suspect it has to do with [gcc using symbols incompatible with JNI](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/10274278/jni-unsatisfiedlinkerror-on-native-method) and me not able to make CMake to do the right thing.

Anyways, it would be nice if we could have the Windows libs along with the executable similar to the other platforms.

issue_title: What do certain settings do - where to look it up?
issue_body:
Hey,

thanks for this amazing toolbox! If it works it will save me so much work. I have intracellular recordings of leech neurons. We injected different electrical current during recording, which elicited action potentials. I try to detect certain spike train characteristics, yet for some stimulus values the toolbox doesn't detect action potentials correctly, yet. I already found out that I can change certain setting using 

`   setDoubleSetting('spike_skipf', 0.1)
    setIntSetting('max_spike_skip', 2)
    setDoubleSetting('Threshold', _settings.threshold)
    setDoubleSetting('DerivativeThreshold', _settings.derivative_threshold)
    setDoubleSetting('interp_step', 0.1)
    setDoubleSetting('burst_factor', 1.5)
    setDoubleSetting('voltage_base_start_perc', 0.9)
    setDoubleSetting('voltage_base_end_perc', 1.0)
    setDoubleSetting("initial_perc", 0.1)
    setDoubleSetting("min_spike_height", 20.0)
    setIntSetting("strict_stiminterval", 0)`

However, instead of randomly changing those values until my peaks are detected correctly, I would like to look up what these settings actually mean. I only found documentation for _Threshold_ and _DerivativeThreshold_. Is there documentation for all setting somewhere? I will upload two plots showing trials in which spikes are detected wrongly or not at all.

![stim_1 0](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/32299254/32717091-b417654c-c858-11e7-9ce0-c389db8c1748.png)

issue_title: Passing extra vars overrides job template vars
issue_body: Passing extra vars with tower-cli overrides job template vars. We have some vars at the inventory level, some at the tower job template and some we would like to pass via tower-cli (jenkins build number in our case). If we pass any extra vars within the tower-cli request, the inventory vars are preserved, while the job template var values are omitted.

issue_title: Passing tags to tower-cli results in started job without tags
issue_body:
Here I launch a new job based on template 272 with the tags default,npm,pm2,env:
```
$ ~/webhook/bin/tower-cli job launch --job-template 272 --tags default,npm,pm2,env -v -f json

But when asking for the job status, you'll see the tags have gone missing (see "job_tags"):

< snip non-interesting json >

```

    "description": "Launch a Job Template:\n\nMake a GET request to this resource to determine if the job_template can be\nlaunched and whether any passwords are required to launch the job_template.\nThe response will include the following fields:\n\n* `ask_variables_on_launch`: Flag indicating whether the job_template is\n  configured to prompt for variables upon launch (boolean, read-only)\n* `can_start_without_user_input`: Flag indicating if the job_template can be\n  launched without user-input (boolean, read-only)\n* `passwords_needed_to_start`: Password names required to launch the\n  job_template (array, read-only)\n* `variables_needed_to_start`: Required variable names required to launch the\n  job_template (array, read-only)\n* `survey_enabled`: Flag indicating if whether the job_template has an enabled\n  survey (boolean, read-only)\n* `credential_needed_to_start`: Flag indicating the presence of a credential\n  associated with the job template.  If not then one should be supplied when\n  launching the job (boolean, read-only)\n\nMake a POST request to this resource to launch the job_template. If any\npasswords or extra variables (extra_vars) are required, they must be passed\nvia POST data, with extra_vars given as a YAML or JSON string and escaped\nparentheses. If `credential_needed_to_start` is `True` then the `credential`\nfield is required as well.\n\nIf successful, the response status code will be 202.  If any required passwords\nare not provided, a 400 status code will be returned.  If the job cannot be\nlaunched, a 405 status code will be returned.",

### Description
Hi - I'm trying to build an api that allows extensions to be added to the API created. For example:

`/v1/service/{serviceid}/{extension}`

Where the method invoked would pass {extension} as the remainder of the URL as a parameter to the invoked code, allowing it to process that part of the URL. 

I understand and have tested in Flask something like this:
`@app.route('/myapp/<serviceId>/<path:extension>', methods = ['POST', 'GET'])`

When I post something like:
`/service/myservice/my/custom/extension`
and as expected get serviceId='myservice' as one parameter and the remainder of the url (e.g. 'my/custom/extension' ) as the value for extension. 

I built what I believe to be equivalent using OpenAPI:
```
paths:
  /service/{serviceid}/{extension}:
    post:
      tags:
      - "serviceextension"
      summary: "Extension endpoint for services""
      operationId: "my_extension_service_by_id"
      produces:
      - "application/json"
      parameters:
      - name: 'serviceid'
        in: path
        type: string
        required: true
      - name: 'extension'
        in: path
        type: string
        required: true
        format: path

```
### Expected behaviour

I expect when posting an URL:

`/service/myservice/my/custom/extension`

I would have the my_extension_service_by_id invoked with  serviceid='myservice' and extension='my/custom/extension'

### Actual behaviour

I get a 404 error

### Steps to reproduce

Create an API as above - I used http://editor.swagger.io/#/ and generated the server stubs using python-flask

### Additional info:
python --version
Python 3.6.0b2

pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"
Version: 1.0.129
Output of the commands:

- `python --version`
- `pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"`

It's well-known that most HTTP routers deals with the trailing slash accordingly, matching both `/foo` or `/foo/` path expressions.

I'm not sure if this was already supported and can be easily enabled programmatically.

### Expected behaviour

Matching the route and reply with a `301` status and redirect it accordingly to `/foo` (however redirection might be optional).

### Actual behaviour

`404` status code is returned instead.

### Steps to reproduce

Just make an HTTP request against a valid endpoint in a connext+Flask app adding a final slash.

### Additional info:

Output of the commands:

- `python 3.5.2`
- `connexion version: 1.0.129`

Hi,

Since two months, I am working on a Django version of Algolia client.
His goal is simple: index (on the fly like [haystack](http://haystacksearch.org/)) the models that has a specified setting, allow to search by model and consume responses very easily.

```python
class MyPony(models.Model):
  ALGOLIA_INDEX_FIELDS = ('name', 'clogs_number',)

  name = models.CharField(max_length=255)
  clogs_number = models.IntegerField()
```

```bash
./manage.py rebuild_algolia_index --model=MyPony
```

```python
>>> import algolia
>>> from my.project.models import MyPony

>>> algolia.search(MyPony, 'Raibow')
AlgoliaResult({'count': 1, 'instances': [<MyPony: Rainbow Dash>], 'model': <class 'my.project.models.MyPony'>, 'page': '1/1'})
```

With a little bit of features like pagination, for work with Django Rest Framework.

```python
import algolia
from algolia.pagination import Paginator as AlgoliaPaginator
from rest_framework import generics

class MyPonySearch(generics.ListAPIView):
    model = MyPony
    serializer_class = MyPonySerializer
    paginator_class = AlgoliaPaginator

    def get_queryset(self):
        return algolia.search(MyPony, self.requests.get('q', ''))
```

You can see all of that in the develop branch of my repo : https://github.com/Kmaschta/django-algolia/tree/develop
(the documentation is not up-to-date)

I learn with joy that you start to make an official Django lib!
But my work becomes obsolete, and work our separate ways is against-productive.

How can we ensure to collaborate about that?

issue_title: Transactions are commited in CommitRequest.NON_TRANSACTIONAL mode
issue_body:
We have just been bitten by this using the latest `0.5.0`. 


We had the following piece of code, run in two different threads:
```python
with dataset.transaction as t:
  entity = dataset.get(entity_key)
  archived_entity = _move_to_archive_key(entity)
  t.delete(entity.key)
  t.put(archived_entity)
  return archived_entity
```

We've seen two copies of the `archived_entity` for the same `entity`.  We've managed to reproduce it with two threads performing this concurrently (running separate Connections each, because httplib2 is not thread safe).

Having used Datastore's underlying technology before, I expected to get a `ConcurrentModificationException` on delete, and one of the threads throwing it, similar to what the Java API for AppEngine describes:
https://cloud.google.com/appengine/docs/java/datastore/transactions#Java_Uses_for_transactions

However, both of them succeed creating two copies of `archived_entity`, and there's no `ConcurrentModificationException` anywhere in `gcloud-python` code.

We've tracked down to the following:

 * `Transaction#commit` calls the `Batch#commit` https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/master/gcloud/datastore/transaction.py#L177
 * `Batch#commit` is unaware of the `Transaction#id`, calls `Connection#commit` without the `transaction_id` parameter https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/master/gcloud/datastore/batch.py#L214
 * `Connection#commit`gets `transaction_id`, and sets the REST request's mode to `datastore_pb.CommitRequest.NON_TRANSACTIONAL` in https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/master/gcloud/datastore/connection.py#L321

*Impact*: This means that **all** transactions made through `python-gcloud` are **non-transactional**. Please treat it as a P0 bug.

issue_title: How to call functions which rely on external modules?
issue_body:
I'm trying to call a function which uses a numpy function in its body, with Pool.map and Pool.map_async, however each time, I get an error saying 'name "numpy" is not defined. I then had a look at the Python 3.5 examples, which use the time module in the body of a function, but this also fails for me. The following are the lines of code I'm executing line by line in Spyder->IPython on Windows 7 x64:

```
from multiprocess import Pool, TimeoutError
from multiprocess import cpu_count as cpuCount, current_process as currentProcess, freeze_support as freezeSupport, active_children as activeChildren

import time, random, sys

def calculate(func, args):
    result = func(*args)
    return '%s says that %s%s = %s' % \
        (currentProcess()._name, func.__name__, args, result)

def mul(a, b):
    time.sleep(0.5*random.random())
    return a * b

pool = Pool(4)
TASKS = [(mul, (i, 7)) for i in range(10)]
results = [pool.apply_async(calculate, t) for t in TASKS]

for r in results:
    print('\t', r.get())
```

That last for loop gives me an error: NameError: name 'time' is not defined

I'm guessing this is because the function mul uses time.sleep, however, the time module is not being loaded in the child pseudo-fork process on Windows. However, this code is straight from the multiprocess github examples, so I'm not sure why this isn't working for me.

Could anyone please help me out? How can you use a function in Pool which uses external modules like time or numpy in that function's body?

issue_title: BaseARN does not generate valid ARNs for region-less resources in China
issue_body:
This is related to #84
S3 bucket ARN cannot contain region information, and the current implementation to generate the ARN partition is based on the region which should not be specified.

This is an issue for buckets in China region (_e.g._ cn-north-1).

This is an example of a valid China bucket: arn:aws-cn:s3:::my_china_bucket

This affects any region-less services like S3 and IAM in China accounts.

aws_partition should be at least optional.
```
def __init__(self, service, resource, region='', account='', aws_partition=''):
    region_string = region.lower()
    if not aws_partition:
        if region_string.startswith("cn-"):
            aws_partition = "aws-cn"
        elif region_string.startswith("us-gov"):
            aws_partition = "aws-us-gov"
        else:
            aws_partition = "aws"

    self.data = "arn:%s:%s:%s:%s:%s" % (
        aws_partition, service, region, account, resource)
```

issue_title: Fix future::Shared and switch back to single-threaded scheduling
issue_body:
To work around https://github.com/alexcrichton/futures-rs/issues/330, we've switched to launching all tasks on a CpuPool. The added overhead of lock contention and context switching into the pool for all Nodes slows things down by about 20%, so we should remove usage of the pool in `graph::Entry::new` once that issue is fixed.

It's possible that #4297 will improve the situation here, because there will be fewer, larger tasks.

issue_title: Enable concurrency by default for zinc
issue_body: The `compile.zinc.worker_count` setting defaults to `1`, despite all known consumers using at least `4` threads by default. This should default to something like `num_cores`.

Currently, [ProductGraph.trace](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/engine/scheduler.py#L271-L288) doesn't include any information from the Node in the trace. Additionally, since Nodes don't enrich the exception on their way up (and generally just do `if type(state) is Throw: return state`), there is a lot of repetition.

Two potential improvements here:
1. Have Nodes create distinct `Throw` instances with their own useful information (ie: "Could not compute X for Y"), rather than simply returning the thrown value.
2.  We should attempt to find the longest useful path to log, and then only log one path. In the case below this worked out, but trace is currently using `product_graph.walk`, so occasionally it will log a _lot_ of different paths.

Example:
```
Exception caught: (<class 'pants.build_graph.address_lookup_error.AddressLookupError'>)

Exception message: Build graph construction failed for DescendantAddresses(directory='mylib'):
  Throw(exc=ResolveError(u'A Struct was not found in namespace mylib/src/test/scala for name "scala". Did you mean one of?:\n  ',))
    Throw(exc=ResolveError(u'A Struct was not found in namespace mylib/src/test/scala for name "scala". Did you mean one of?:\n  ',))
      Throw(exc=ResolveError(u'A Struct was not found in namespace mylib/src/test/scala for name "scala". Did you mean one of?:\n  ',))
        Throw(exc=ResolveError(u'A Struct was not found in namespace mylib/src/test/scala for name "scala". Did you mean one of?:\n  ',))
          Throw(exc=ResolveError(u'A Struct was not found in namespace mylib/src/test/scala for name "scala". Did you mean one of?:\n  ',))
            Throw(exc=ResolveError(u'A Struct was not found in namespace mylib/src/test/scala for name "scala". Did you mean one of?:\n  ',))
```
PyYAML is more or less unmaintained at this point and ruaml.yaml has some bugfixes and improvements.

issue_body:
FreeImage is maintained by a small group of devs, which is not necessarily a bad thing, but they are on sourceforge with CVS for version control. On [people asking]( http://sourceforge.net/p/freeimage/discussion/36111/thread/f2fae857/ ) them to move to Github, so they can have CI, more contributors, etc. they seem to fail to see the advantage.

This is slightly worrisome, and we should keep an eye out for alternatives, thus this issue.

* Nathanael Jones keeps a clone of freeimage. They have not changed code, but added CI: https://github.com/imazen/freeimage
* There's several other Freeimage clones on GH, but most seem to have changed the code.
* There is https://github.com/OpenImageIO/oiio
* We could ctypes into libjpeg and libpng (#111)

issue_title: broken auth on payloads containing tilde chars
issue_body:
client.py uses urllib.urlencode, which percent-encodes the tilde character (~) as %7E in order to generate the signature. ~ isn't really a reserved character, so this isn't guaranteed consistent with other URI libraries.

On the other hand, the base_url is encoded via requests in which [the tilde issue](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/issues/369#issuecomment-3953802) has [been corrected](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/pull/426/files#diff-5956087d5835a57d9ef6fff974f6fd9bR399) and no longer matches urllib's weird behavior.

Ultimately this results in a 403 error (client id omitted below):
```
"Unable to authenticate the request. Provided 'signature' is not valid for the provided client ID, or the provided 'client' is not valid.
The signature was checked against the URL: /maps/api/geocode/json?address=560+Maple+Street+South+%2310~&components=administrative_area%3AMN%7Clocality%3AWaconia%7Cpostal_code%3A55387&client=
If this does not match the URL you requested, please ensure that your request is URL encoded correctly. Learn more: https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/business/webservices/auth"
```

Although tildes should be rare, ideal behavior would probably be either:

1. Use the same encoding library for the base_url and for computing the authed_url, or
2. Raise an informative exception on disallowed characters (incl. tilde)

instead of waiting for a non-specific 403.

issue_title: Exposing lower level model evaulation data
issue_body:
Thanks for all the hard work on this! Parserator has definitely made it easy to create a model with crfsuite. As I dig into fine tuning my model, I'd like to have access to the metrics provided by crfsuite (accuracy, precision, recall). 

It looks the python wrapper does provide access to this data (https://github.com/scrapinghub/python-crfsuite/issues/42#issuecomment-227805517), what do you think of a PR that exposes this as a return value of [`trainModel`](https://github.com/datamade/parserator/blob/master/parserator/training.py#L29)?

Parserator starts lagging when training on large documents -- and when parsing large documents. It would be nice if Parserator used some of the Python parallelization modules to take advantage of multiple cores, when available. Right now, I'm working around this (somewhat) using the parallel command. But it would be great if you could just pass parserator a -P flag and it would parallelize for you.

    parallel python parse_documents.py ::: $(find path/to/corpus/*_text.txt -type f)

All text returned by soco should unicode objects. get_current_track_info does not seem to respect that, as can be seen from the two python 2.7 example outputs below:

```python
{u'album': 'Everything Glows', u'artist': 'D-A-D', u'title': 'As Common As', u'uri': 'x-file-cifs://TLE-SERVER/share/ogg/D-A-D%20-%20Everything%20Glows/11%20-%20As%20Common%20As.ogg', u'playlist_position': '11', u'duration': '0:04:23', u'position': '0:02:47', u'album_art': u''}
# ... and ...
{u'album': u'', u'artist': u'', u'title': '17.mp3', u'uri': 'http://www.noiseaddicts.com/samples/17.mp3', u'playlist_position': '12', u'duration': '0:00:04', u'position': '0:00:03', u'album_art': u''}
```

Ideally I think, that when the radio stations has been brough into the data structures fold, then this method should return data structure objects according to their type, but for now, this should be fixable by turning all strings into unicode after they have been pulled out of the XML.

Publish and test
