issue_title: Project template: 'gulp scripts' is behaving unexpectedly
issue_body:
The `scripts` task in the project template is behaving incorrectly. When `gulp scripts` is run, the task never exits.

This breaks automated deployment, specifically, it stops us from adding `gulp scripts` to [`manage.py update`](https://github.com/onespacemedia/server-management/blob/master/server_management/management/commands/update.py), forcing us to run `manage.py update`, then ssh to the server and run `gulp scripts` interactively, kill it, then run `collectstatic`.

The correct behaviour would be for `gulp scripts` to work the same as `gulp styles`: exit after generating files.

issue_title: All values in lxc_conf are passed to docker-egine as string
issue_body:
Hi,

I know that LXC will be deprecated, but I've noticed a small bug: all values in lxc_conf passed in host_config when creating containers are converted to string. This causes some issues with options like 'lxc.cgroup.cpu.shares' as docker-engine expects an int when unmarshalling the json, so any value expected as non-string from the docker-engine side will fail:

```
ERRO[173466] Handler for POST /v1.21/containers/create returned error: json: cannot unmarshal string into Go value of type int64 
ERRO[173466] HTTP Error                                    err=json: cannot unmarshal string into Go value of type int64 statusCode=500
```

The issue is in utils/utils.py, L624:
```
    if isinstance(lxc_conf, dict):
        formatted = []
        for k, v in six.iteritems(lxc_conf):
            formatted.append({'Key': k, 'Value': str(v)})
        lxc_conf = formatted
```

A quick and dirty solution would be to replace `str(v)` for `v` so the decision on the type used would recall on the user. 

Otherwise, we could go farther and map the types expected from docker-engine for every `lxc-conf` value in order to match the docker API.

Tested on this gear:
```
$ pip freeze | grep docker-py && python --version && docker version && uname -a && lsb_release -a

Python 2.7.6

```

I could try to provide a pull request if necessary.

Thanks!

2.3.0 is the latest documented release @ http://docker-py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/images.html , but 2.4.2 is what is available @ https://pypi.python.org/pypi/docker/

issue_title: Template for session method is not DRY
issue_body:
build/templates/session.py.mako
src/nimodinst/templates/session.py.mako

... are pretty similar. One of the trickiest parts in those templates is where we render the code for a method.

Turns out that in `build/templates/session.py.mako` we already broke that up into a mako def called `render_method`.

We should eliminate duplication and put that piece of template its own file and use it in both places. There's a good chance it will also apply to NI-TClk which will likely have it's own session template.

issue_title: ELMo: The representation of two layers are the same
issue_body:
When I fed a sentence to ELMo following the instruction, I got two representation of two-layer LSTM hidden, but I found, for each word, its two-layer hidden vectors are identical.

**To Reproduce**
`from allennlp.modules.elmo import Elmo, batch_to_ids`
`options_file = "https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/allennlp/models/elmo/2x4096_512_2048cnn_2xhighway/elmo_2x4096_512_2048cnn_2xhighway_options.json"`

issue_title: Expose list of supported schemes and formats?
issue_body:
# Overview

`tabulator` clients prefer to have an access to supported schemes and formats programmatically.

# Ideas

It could be something like:

```
tabulator.SCHEMES -> list
tabulator.FORMATS -> list
```

You should be able to do:

```
dp = DataPackage()

# does inference automatically if this is CSV etc etc
# nice option would be to copy the file into the data package directory (if that is known)
dp.add_resource('path-to-file')

# should have schema etc ...
dp.to_json()
```

Currently quite verbose e.g.

```
# dan to add
```

From what I've been able to gather, using Kubernetes as a backend for Deis means that the Deis setup scripts will actually create a Kubernetes cluster to work with.

What about coupling Deis to an existing cluster?

We currently have a k8s cluster for our application needs, but some people (including myself) are interested in improving their deployment/management flow of services by using Deis a management interface in front of the k8s cluster for some of our applications.

Having Deis schedule the services in our existing k8s cluster would be great, having a second k8s to take care of, not so much.

issue_title: PV clock drift causes more ceph havok
issue_body:
I am running nothing but CoreOS 607 instances now, in both of my environments.

While load testing our 3-node staging cluster, the cluster was a bit slow. Looking into the problem, I noticed that one of these PV based instances had drifted 10-15 seconds into the future. I turned up ntp and synced the times. Not more than 15 minutes later, two of our instances became non-responsive. After rebooting both instances from the AWS console, we stopped the load testing, and I spent a couple of hours piecing back together the fleet units that had fallen over - repairing ceph in the process.

Why there is no support for Arabic language? (cp1256)
issue_body:
I just found a test for Arabic.
https://github.com/chardet/chardet/blob/master/tests/windows-1256-arabic/_chromium_windows-1256_with_no_encoding_specified.html

No handlers could be found for logger "tsfresh.feature_selection.feature_selector"

Does it matters?

Hi blueyonder

It would be nice to be able to have a [PEP343 ](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0343/) compliant turbodbc connection.

This way, I could write something like:
`
with turbodbc.connect('...') as conn:
`

Thank you for your useful and great project

### What is wrong?
Discussion started in #326 which are about how to supply `salt` value to contract-creating transaction in Sharding where `CREATE2` style contract creation is implemented. `CREATE2` takes in a value `salt` and init code `code`, address of the new contract is derived from hashing these two values together: `sha3(salt | code)`.

In the short term,  we can simply add a `salt` field in transaction format, but this field is only useful in contract creation transactions. [EIP232](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/issues/232) provides a more elastic format which adopts the notion of a version(or format) number. With EIP232 we can assign different type of transaction with different version/format number which corresponds to different transaction format.

### How can it be fixed

Implement EIP232 in Sharding.
@pipermerriam provides a rough sketch of the possible implementation in the thread(https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/issues/326#issuecomment-362714619):
Quote:
```python
class EIP232Transaction(rlp.Serializable):
    fields = (
        ('version', ...),
        ('inner_data', sedes.binary),
    (
    transaction_formats = {
        1: TransactionFormatA,
        2: TransactionFormatB,
    }

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.txn_data = rlp.decode(self.inner_data, sedes=self.transaction_formats[self.version])

    def __getattr__(self, name):
        return getattr(self.txn_data, name)
```
> We have an outer transaction class which contains a mapping of version numbers to inner transaction classes. Then, during initialization, we decode the outer data packet, and then lookup the appropriate inner transaction class and use it to decode the inner data packet. Then we put some convenience APIs on the outer class to allow direct property access to the inner transaction properties.

issue_title: Shard syncer tests are nearly impossible to debug and take a long time to run
issue_body:
So, I didn't notice these new tests until they were merged, and I only did because my latest PR (#684) introduced some changes to `p2p.Server` that caused those tests to break. Unfortunately, they are quite complex, high-level, integration tests that give no visibility into what's going on under the hood, so when they break it is a nightmare to debug. Also, [they take a really long time to run](https://circleci.com/gh/ethereum/py-evm/8621?utm_campaign=vcs-integration-link&utm_medium=referral&utm_source=github-build-link):

> 19.36s call     tests/p2p/test_sharding.py::test_shard_syncer[3-connections1]
> 18.91s call     tests/p2p/test_sharding.py::test_shard_syncer[3-connections2]
> 10.41s call     tests/p2p/test_sharding.py::test_shard_syncer[2-connections0]

At this point, I understand writing unit tests for those parts will be tricky, but having a complex integration test like this as part of our CI is likely to slow down development of p2p-related code significantly -- I just spent more than 1h to get them to pass again. I think we'd be better off skipping those tests in CI, the same way we do for the tests in `tests/p2p/test_lightchain_integration.py`

### What is wrong?

```python
   ERROR  05-23 16:16:10  base_events  Task exception was never retrieved
future: <Task finished coro=<PeerPool.lookup_random_node() done, defined at /Users/piper/sites/py-evm/p2p/peer.py:741> exception=AlreadyWaiting("There's another coroutine waiting for a neighbours packet from <Node(0x9124@148.251.19.50)>",)>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/piper/sites/py-evm/p2p/peer.py", line 745, in lookup_random_node
    await self.discovery.lookup_random(self.cancel_token)
  File "/Users/piper/sites/py-evm/p2p/discovery.py", line 93, in lookup_random
    return await self.kademlia.lookup_random(self.cancel_token.chain(cancel_token))
  File "/Users/piper/sites/py-evm/p2p/kademlia.py", line 575, in lookup_random
    return await self.lookup(random.randint(0, k_max_node_id), cancel_token)
  File "/Users/piper/sites/py-evm/p2p/kademlia.py", line 565, in lookup
    *[_find_node(node_id, n) for n in nodes_to_ask])
  File "/Users/piper/sites/py-evm/p2p/kademlia.py", line 541, in _find_node
    candidates = await self.wait_neighbours(remote, cancel_token)
  File "/Users/piper/sites/py-evm/p2p/kademlia.py", line 461, in wait_neighbours
    "There's another coroutine waiting for a neighbours packet from {}".format(remote))
p2p.kademlia.AlreadyWaiting: There's another coroutine waiting for a neighbours packet from <Node(0x9124@148.251.19.50)>
```

looks like a race condition of some kind which results in stacktrace being dumped to terminal.

* Populus Version: on  master
* OS: osx


### What was wrong?
https://medium.com/@pipermerriam/dropping-python-2-support-d781e7b48160 says that populus will drop support for  `python2.7`. Also `py-solc` dropped support for `python2.7` in https://github.com/ethereum/py-solc/pull/54. `py-solc` being a dependency of `populus`, the tests are failing as seen in #464

### How can it be fixed?
Quick fix: Drop `python2.7` tests from CI.
Long fix: Modify code to support python3 only syntax.

issue_title: Anaconda installer `.bashrc` modifications cannot be easily reversed
issue_body:
(Filing against conda as I can't find a public issue tracker for the Anaconda installer)

In helping out an attendee at a Software Carpentry bootcamp that had run into issue #1411, I found that Anaconda had added a line to the end of the attendee's `.bashrc` file that placed Anaconda's `bin` directory at the head of `PATH`. This meant that *all* affected commands were shadowed in *every* terminal session, in a way that *couldn't* be easily reverted via:

    source `which deactivate`

The additions to the user's shell config when they instruct the installer to enable the use of Anaconda by default should either:

1. Activate the main environment properly so that it can be deactivated as needed (currently attempting to deactivate claims that it isn't activated); or
2. Be more selective about which binary commands `conda` shadows, since losing access to the system versions without manually editing PATH can pose a significant problem, especially when there's a problem with the way the conda provided version is built (as appears to be the case in #1411)

issue_title: invalid conda sub-commands do not report error
issue_body:
If I mis-type a conda sub-command I do not get an error message, I only get a non-zero exit code.

Say I wanted to do:

```
conda create -q -n myenv python=3.6 pandas
```

but instead I fat-fingered it and wrote:

```
conda creat -q -n myenv python=3.6 pandas
```

Or put that in a script.

If I checked the error code (`echo $?`) I'd see that the spelling mistake resulted in an exit code of 1.  But if I only look at the output, and don't see any (thanks to `-q` in this case) then I'd be surprised that the command hadn't actually done anything.

-parmap->python 2.7*
-python 3.6*

issue_title: Compatibility with locally built packages and anaconda?
issue_body:
I've been having lots of issues building my own packages on Arch Linux. It seems that Anaconda, having priority on my path, is providing with the build tools before the system's kick in. I've gotten conflicts with `make`, `qmake`, and several libraries. 

Are there some best practices to handle this conflicts? Is there something that can be added to conda to make this better? I mean a command to modify the path priority? How do you guys handle these types of issues?

This is a snippet of one of the errors:

`-- Configuring done
CMake Warning at CMakeLists.txt:46 (add_library):
  Cannot generate a safe runtime search path for target image_view because
  files in some directories may conflict with libraries in implicit
  directories:

    runtime library [libglib-2.0.so.0] in /usr/lib64 may be hidden by files in:
      /home/mimoralea/anaconda3/lib
    runtime library [libgobject-2.0.so.0] in /usr/lib64 may be hidden by files in:
      /home/mimoralea/anaconda3/lib
    runtime library [libgio-2.0.so.0] in /usr/lib64 may be hidden by files in:
      /home/mimoralea/anaconda3/lib
    runtime library [libgthread-2.0.so.0] in /usr/lib64 may be hidden by files in:
      /home/mimoralea/anaconda3/lib
    runtime library [libgmodule-2.0.so.0] in /usr/lib64 may be hidden by files in:
      /home/mimoralea/anaconda3/lib
    runtime library [libcairo.so.2] in /usr/lib64 may be hidden by files in:
      /home/mimoralea/anaconda3/lib
    runtime library [libpango-1.0.so.0] in /usr/lib64 may be hidden by files in:
      /home/mimoralea/anaconda3/lib
    runtime library [libpangocairo-1.0.so.0] in /usr/lib64 may be hidden by files in:
      /home/mimoralea/anaconda3/lib

      /home/mimoralea/anaconda3/lib

  Some of these libraries may not be found correctly.
`

In the end, I just temporarily removed `conda` from the PATH while I install some software, but this seems kind of hacky. Please, advise.

issue_title: Conda does not respect exact matchspecs
issue_body:
Really strange here, but I've followed this as far as I can in conda-build, and I think it's in conda.

I have these specs:

```
ipdb> sub_build_ms_deps
[MatchSpec('openssl 1.0.2k 1'), MatchSpec('zlib 1.2.8 3'), MatchSpec('bzip2 1.0.6 3'), MatchSpec('ncurses 5.9 10'), MatchSpec('curl 7.52.1 0'), MatchSpec('llvm 4.0.0 h95a1600_0'), MatchSpec('cmake 3.6.3 0'), MatchSpec('xz 5.2.2 1'), MatchSpec('expat 2.1.0 0')]
```

These get fed into conda's install_actions function here: https://github.com/conda/conda-build/blob/master/conda_build/environ.py#L655

```
ipdb> n
> /home/msarahan/conda-build/conda_build/build.py(1042)build()
   1041                     index, index_timestamp = get_build_index(m.config, m.config.build_subdir)
-> 1042                     build_actions = environ.get_install_actions(m.config.build_prefix, index,
   1043                                                                 sub_build_ms_deps, m.config,
```

Now I'm not 100% certain that the package exists in repodata yet.  I know that it does on disk, but not sure about in-memory.  It should, but I'm not certain.

These don't implement everything we need.  In particular, they don't really directly capture things like glibc version.  versions.yml is currently limited solely to the few packages that conda-build has hard-coded.  It really should support arbitrary packages.  This ties into https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1142

issue_title: `conda install --file` reports Python 3.5* conflict for everything
issue_body:
I have a file with a bunch of packages listed in it (attached, below).  No versions.  I want to install them into my current Python 3.5-based Conda environment using the command:

```
$ conda install --file pkgs_only.txt
```

[pkgs_only.txt](https://github.com/conda/conda/files/325862/pkgs_only.txt)

However I get this kind of output:

```
$ conda install --file pkgs_only.txt
Fetching package metadata .......
Solving package specifications: ....

The following specifications were found to be in conflict:
  - astropy
  - python 3.5*
Use "conda info <package>" to see the dependencies for each package.
```

I don't understand how `astropy` can be in conflict with `python 3.5`.  The environment already has `astropy` and `python` 3.5 installed.  Is that the problem?  Is this a variation on the problem I reported in #2598?

issue_title: Add a "show aliases" feature, or an --is-alias option to index selection
issue_body:
We have issues when an alias spans over open and closed indices. Because of this, I have to remove the indices from the alias before I close them. To do this, I need a list of all existing aliases. I do this with curl and the _cat/aliases API currently, but the rest of the script uses curator, so that would be more consistent.
The most general implementation would probably be to add an "--is-alias" option to the index selection.

issue_title: [ACS] Add a browse/dashboard command for Kubernetes
issue_body: The `az acs dcos browse` command allows easily connecting to the admin dashboard for a DC/OS cluster in ACS, however, there isn't currently an equivalent for Kubernetes. It would be great if there was an `az acs kubernetes browse` command that handled starting the local proxy and launching a browser to the k8s admin UI. This would behave similar to the `minikube dashboard` command (which many may use for local development), and would save users a few steps.

issue_title: Suggestion: Prompt for confirmation on disk delete
issue_body:
When deleting a disk with `az disk delete`, there is no prompt that you wish to continue. When deleting a resource group with `az group delete`, you are prompted or can pass `--yes` to continue without a prompt.

Deleting an NSG rule with `az nsg rule delete` also doesn't prompt for confirmation, though deleting a disk is potentially a more destructive process. There are likely other similar examples.

I'd recommend that at the least, a confirmation is added to `az disk delete`, and ideally some amount of consistency across potentially destructive commands that delete resources.

issue_title: Insert space after # in block comments of the file headers
issue_body:
This is today's copyright header“
```
#---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Copyright (c) Microsoft Corporation. All rights reserved.
# Licensed under the MIT License. See License.txt in the project root for license information.
#---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```
There isn't a space between the `-` and `#`. It is not recommended in [PEP8](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#block-comments). Errors are reported in PyCharm and other editors if PEP8 is enabled.

The files can be updated together in one batch automatically.

issue_title: az keyvault download returns incorrect exit code if not logged in
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
If I try to download a secret from the azure keyvault, but I am not logged in. The process returns with no error message and a exit code of `0`.

**To Reproduce**
1. Delete (or move) your `.azure` credentials folder
2. Run `az keyvault secret download --vault-name <your-vault-name> -n <secret-name> -e base64 -f <filename>

**Expected behavior**
After running the above command without any azure credentials, the command should return with a non-zero exit code. Ideally, it would also print to `stdout` suggesting I should execute `az login`.

**Environment summary**
Running on `OSX 10.13.5` with `az 2.0.35`

This was a very important feature from `azure-xplat-cli` for me.

The fact that all commands took `--subscription-id` means that I can write scripts that have the user input their subscription ID and I can take action in that subscription without needing to change the global state.

Without this functionality, it's seems impossible to have concurrent running scripts that are running in different subscriptions.

See CLI:
https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/439e0e09702ef10f87d86b093b788c24e92169c2/src/azure-cli-core/azure/cli/core/commands/client_factory.py#L86

Should probably be done in the pipeline PR directly #106

issue_title: Fails in Python 3.4 and older due to circular dependency between types.py and utils.py
issue_body: The lines `from uamqp import types` in utils.py and `from uamqp import utils` in types.py are causing a circular dependency that doesn't work in Python 3.4 (but seems to be okay in 3.5 and 3.6). It appears this can be fixed by changing the lines to `import types` and `import utils`, although I'm not sure if that fix will still work in 3.5 and 3.6.

issue_title: When configured to use 'copy' file_action pulsar still attempts http transfer
issue_body:
Hi John - I emailed about this earlier, but did some more digging. I'm guessing my hacks on the code in the diffs below break things in other setups, but they are letting the 'copy' file_action complete.

Tried to configure pulsar with a destination as follows:

```xml
<destination id="pulsar_rest_dest" runner="pulsar_rest">
            <param id="url">http://localhost:3032/</param>
            <param id="private_token">xxxxxx</param>
            <param id="default_file_action">copy</param>
            <param id="submit_user">$__user_name__</param>
            <param id="submit_native_specification">--partition=super</param>
            <param id="file_action_config">/project/apps/galaxy/galaxy/config/pulsar_actions.yaml</param>
        </destination>
```
Job submission always failed. Found pulsar was trying to upload files via http, not copy them. Worked through call trace and found that action_type is being picked up correctly, but is not being passed into the client.put_file method. This method has a default action_type in it's def which means http transfer was always used.

Modified to pass the action_type through with diff:

```diff
--- a/lib/pulsar/client/staging/up.py
+++ b/lib/pulsar/client/staging/up.py
@@ -348,8 +348,7 @@ class TransferTracker(object):
         if action.staging_needed:
             local_action = action.staging_action_local
             if local_action:
-                response = self.client.put_file(path, type, name=name, contents=contents)
-
+                response = self.client.put_file(path, type, name=name, contents=contents, action_type=action.action_type)
                 def get_path():
                     return response['path']
             else:

```
It then attempted to copy files, but was failing in the shutil copy call as the path obtained from pulsar is a json structure, not a bare string path. abspath calls mangling it into a combination of the GALAXY_HOME and the json structure:

```

  File "/project/apps/galaxy/galaxy/lib/pulsar/client/staging/up.py", line 22, in submit_job
    file_stager = FileStager(client, client_job_description, job_config)
  File "/project/apps/galaxy/galaxy/lib/pulsar/client/staging/up.py", line 91, in __init__
    self.__upload_input_files()
  File "/project/apps/galaxy/galaxy/lib/pulsar/client/staging/up.py", line 160, in __upload_input_files
    self.__upload_input_file(input_file)
  File "/project/apps/galaxy/galaxy/lib/pulsar/client/staging/up.py", line 166, in __upload_input_file
    self.transfer_tracker.handle_transfer(input_file, path_type.INPUT)
  File "/project/apps/galaxy/galaxy/lib/pulsar/client/staging/up.py", line 351, in handle_transfer
    response = self.client.put_file(path, type, name=name, contents=contents, action_type=action.action_type)
  File "/project/apps/galaxy/galaxy/lib/pulsar/client/client.py", line 177, in put_file
    copy(path, pulsar_path)
  File "/project/apps/galaxy/galaxy/lib/pulsar/client/util.py", line 27, in copy
    shutil.copyfile(source, destination)
  File "/project/apps/galaxy/.pyenv/versions/2.7.8/lib/python2.7/shutil.py", line 83, in copyfile
    with open(dst, 'wb') as fdst:
IOError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: '/project/apps/galaxy/galaxy/{"path": "/project/apps/galaxy/pulsar/files/staging/d283a74499144b89ae73666463c2bc73/inputs/dataset_91.dat"}'
```

Tried to fix this with a naive patch:

```diff
--- a/lib/pulsar/client/client.py
+++ b/lib/pulsar/client/client.py
@@ -1,5 +1,5 @@
 import os
-from json import dumps
+from json import dumps, loads

 from .destination import submit_params
 from .setup_handler import build as build_setup_handler
@@ -173,7 +173,7 @@ class JobClient(BaseJobClient):
         if action_type == 'transfer':
             return self._upload_file(args, contents, input_path)
         elif action_type == 'copy':
-            pulsar_path = self._raw_execute('path', args)
+            pulsar_path = loads(self._raw_execute('path', args))['path']
             copy(path, pulsar_path)
             return {'path': pulsar_path}

```

It's now staging files by copying. I'm not sure how much other stuff these diffs are likely to break though.

issue_title: Values are not properly encoded in URLs
issue_body:
Hi,

For a given metric `name` or dimension `key/value` the fields are simply joined with a `/` instead of encoding it before, and then joining it.

Example:
A dimension with a key `AwsUniqueId` and value `applicationelb_app/myalb/id` will send a PUT request upon calling `dimension_update` to the URL `https://api.signalfx.com/v2/dimension/AWSUniqueId/applicationelb_app/myalb/id` instead of `https://api.signalfx.com/v2/dimension/AWSUniqueId/applicationelb_app%2Fmyalb%2Fid`.

Workaround for given example is possible by monkey patching the object instance:
```python
import urllib.parse
import types
import signalfx

def monkeypatch_update_dimension(self, key, value, description=None,
                                 custom_properties=None, tags=None, **kwargs):
    '''monkeypatch dimension update'''
    data = {'description': description or '',
            'customProperties': custom_properties or {},
            'tags': tags or [],
            'key': key,
            'value': value}

    resp = self._put(self._u(self._DIMENSION_ENDPOINT_SUFFIX,
                             urllib.parse.quote(key, safe=''),
                             urllib.parse.quote(value, safe='')),
                     data=data, **kwargs)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

def main():
    sfx = signalfx.SignalFx()
    rest = sfx.rest(args.token)
    rest.update_dimension = types.MethodType(monkeypatch_update_dimension, rest)
```

This only solves the use case provided in the example, but I believe the same would apply to other methods as well. I unfortunately do not have the time to write a proper pull request for all methods that would need patching, but I believe the latter should provide enough information for a fix.

I was thinking it would be more elegant to patch the `_u` function, but since the endpoints have slashes inside that actually need to be non-encoded, it would not really work to apply the encoding to all arguments.

Let me know if you need any more info.

issue_body:
When a message is sent to the logging system, that message must be handled some how. A typical method of handling a logging message is to simply print it to stderr, but other handlers might send the message to a file, fire off an email, emit an SMTP message or anything else you can dream up. The logging system is organized in to an arbitrarily complex hierarchy, and messages can be sent to anywhere in this hierarchy. In NailGun's case, the logging hierarchy is quite simple:

    nailgun
    └── client

However, it may be expanded in the future:

    nailgun
    ├── client
    ├── entity_fields
    ├── entity_mixins
    └── entities

When a message is sent to any element in this logging hierarchy, the message is propagated up the hierarchy, and at each level, the message may be used by one or more handlers.

Currently, NailGun has no logging handlers. Thus, when the `nailgun.client` logging element a logging message, that message is handed up to `nailgun` logging element, and it is then discarded. Currently, when a message is logged, Python 2 prints out this warning:

> No handlers could be found for logger "nailgun.client"

And Python 3 prints out a message like this:

> Received HTTP 422 response: {
>   "error": {"id":null,"errors":{"name":["can't be blank"]},"full_messages":["DNS domain can't be blank"]}
> }

Neither of these are ideal.

According to the documentation, the logging system is automatically configured with default values when functions such as `logging.warning` and `logging.debug` are called. However, NailGun does not do that. (Nor does Robottelo!) Instead, _methods_ named `warning`, `debug`, etc. are called.

NailGun's logging system should be initialized so that error messages are properly logged.

```python
packages=find_packages(exclude=("tests", "tests.*",)),
```

issue_title: Each dashboard should have its own class_id for the svg image

issue_body:
With #409 merged, the usage of `mstridx` is now uniform.  There are three places with:

```
ymd.append(value)
assert mstridx == -1
mstridx = len(ymd)-1
```

I propose that `mstridx` should be an attribute of `_ymd`.  My preferred implementation is a new `_ymd` method that gets called in some cases instead of `append`:

```
   def set_month(self, value, idx):
           assert self.mstridx is None # or -1 in in the current usage
           self.append(value)
           self.mstridx = len(self) - 1
           self._midx = idx  # This is new, will be discussed below
```

Then in `_parse`, the append + assert + set-mstridx becomes `ymd.set_month(value, i)`.  Advantages:

- One fewer state variable to carry around the `_parse` loop. This will make it simpler to refactor that into smaller functions.
- `_ymd.resolve_ymd` no longer needs `mstridx` as an argument.
- Analogous methods for `set_year` and `set_day` allow for similar validation (e.g. calling `ymd.append(info.convertyear(...))` to implicitly specify `ystridx`)

The new attribute `_midx`, and the analogous `_yidx, _didx` are there so that we can recover information later.  Keeping track of absolute position in addition to relative position may be important in cases where we need to back-track for disambiguation (see #394).  This may also be helpful for addressing #125.


Related: instead of carrying around `skipped_tokens` and `last_skipped_token_i`, use a set `skipped_indices`.  The re-combining can be done at the end of the parsing, `last_skipped_token_i` is easy to get, and we retain information that can be useful in potential post-processing.

There is a bug in rrule.py (dateutil 2.3). The `while True` loop on line 659 does not always terminate:
```python
elif freq == HOURLY:
    if filtered:
        # Jump to one iteration before next day
        hour += ((23-hour)//interval)*interval
    while True:
        hour += interval
        div, mod = divmod(hour, 24)
        if div:
            hour = mod
            day += div
            fixday = True
        if not byhour or hour in byhour:
            break
```
Here's an example:
If the initial value of `hour` is an odd number, say `23`, and the `byhour` tuple is, e.g., `(0, 6, 12, 18)`, then we have an endless loop if `interval` is even because `hour` will always be odd and, hence, `hour in byhour` is never `True`!

Because dateutil is used by matplotlib, this causes some plotting routines to freeze.

So, if you're not familiar with it, here are the current `FS.openbin` docstring:

* in plain RestructuredText:
   ```rst
     Open a binary file-like object.
    
    :param str path: A path on the filesystem.
    :param str mode: Mode to open file (must be a valid non-text
        mode). Since this method only opens binary files, the `b` in
        the mode string is implied.
    :param buffering: Buffering policy (-1  to use default
        buffering, 0 to disable buffering, or positive integer to
        indicate buffer size).
    :type buffering: int
    :param options: Keyword parameters for any additional
        information required by the filesystem (if any).
    :rtype: file object
   
    :raises fs.errors.FileExpected: If the path is not a file.
    :raises fs.errors.FileExists: If the file exists, and
        *exclusive mode* is specified (`x` in the mode).
    :raises fs.errors.ResourceNotFound: If `path` does not exist.
    ```
* in Numpy style (takes more vertical space):
  ```rst
    Open a binary file-like object.

    Arguments
    ---------
    path : str
        a path on the filesystem.

        mode to open file (must be a valid, non-text

        if the file exists and *exclusive mode* is

        if `path` does not exist
    ```

* in Google style (takes more horizontal space, my favorite):

issue_title: The Magnificent AutoBlob(tm): Use heuristics, magic tricks, binwalk, etc to automate blob loading
issue_labels: [{'id': 216797116, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/angr/cle/labels/enhancement', 'name': 'enhancement', 'color': '84b6eb', 'default': True}, {'id': 720784428, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/angr/cle/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': '4dcc5c', 'default': True}, {'id': 216797117, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/angr/cle/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': '159818', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
Do you work a lot with blobs? I sure do, and I can't stand what a pain it is to use the Blob backend.  
There's been a lot of work out there on automatically figuring out what your blob is.  We can and should leverage it, to make this a mostly-automatic process.

This is a tracking issue for the effort to implement this functionality as a CLE backend, so that we can handle as much as possible automagically.  Short-term goals include support for "clean" blobs, that is, in-tact blobs ready to flash to a microcontroller, but longer-term we'd like to support nasty stuff, like code extracted from real systems in a not-very-precise way, ripping the code right out of a Windows flash tool binary, and so on.

Want to help? Working on this doesn't require much/any knowledge about the rest of the angr family of libraries, so it's great for those newer to the project. Read on...

Currently, CLE will try all possible backends until it finds one that works. (or fails, and tells you to go try the Blob loader manually) AutoBlob should be able more or less guess the parameters that would be needed to use Blob, and load the binary for you if it can.

While many backends load all kinds of fancy metadata, symbols, and what have you, you always need at least these three things around, in order to load a binary:
- Architecture: Obviously you need to know what arch and endness the binary is.  The more specific we can get, the better. (note: some day we will have better embedded architecture support, and being "more specific" to a sub-architecture will be actually important)

- Base address: Where in memory does this blob belong? (but if we are sure it won't matter, we can make one up)

- Entry point: What should angr consider to be the beginning of the program?

But how do we get those?
Blobs are not all as opaque as we may think.  There are typically bits and pieces of metadata around that we may be able to find and use.  Let's give an example:
ARM microcontroller blobs, that is, totally unpacked, fully-formed, clean ARM blobs, will contain, somewhere, the initial Interrupt Vector Table (or Exception Vector Table, if you will).  If you're lucky, and your blob is extra clean, it'll be the very first thing in the file. This gives us a surprising amount of information; the very first word in the table is actually the initial stack pointer, which will fall into the normal physical address range for RAM (the low 0x20000000's).  The endness of this value tells us, of course, the endness.  Let's also assume if we see a pointer in about the right place that falls in that range using either endness, that it's an ARM blob (but we will refine this guess later)  The second word is the IRQ handler for IRQ 1, "reset", which is taken always at power-on.  That's our entry point! We're done here.
MSP430 binaries have a similar thing. although this time, the IVT is at the very top end of memory, and goes backward.  Same deal there.

IVT's aren't the only way to figure it out; angr has two existing analyses (girlscout and boyscout) which help with this kind of identification. There is a lot of uncertainty as to how well they work, and they can probably stand to be retrofitted, cleaned, and reapplied inside of CLE.

There's also the very popular tool binwalk, a python library/utility/thingy for carving stuff out of other stuff in a pretty reasonable way.  It's a bit more exciting than just libmagic on steroids, there's disassembly-based analysis, specific support for weird firmware formats, compression, etc etc.
There's even a pile of other tools based on binwalk (like firmware_mod_kit) for playing with firmware. Can we leverage any of this to make educated guesses about our blobs?

Here's what we need to do:
- Implement the initial AutoBlob backend.  

- Create a framework for adding identification functions. deas.
This divides our "magic tricks" into two flavors: _initial_ and _secondary_.
initial means that this is performed to merely get the blob into CLE at all; we use this as part of the is_compatible() method for this backend to tell if AutoBlob will even work.  We call it again in the constructor to actually load the thing. "secondary" techniques let us refine our initial guesses, such as detecting sub-architectures, reorganizing the memory map (what's code, what's data, what goes where), or maybe detecting the OS (is this blob actually VXWorks? Great, we should know that)

- Retrofit boyscout and girlscout as catch-all techniques.  This will require dealing with the fact that these are angr analyses, and we cannot have cle depend on angr itself; We may be able to get away with it using a "soft dependency" (only try those if angr is around and works)
There are issues open about fixing these (like https://github.com/angr/angr/pull/449)

- Modify CLE's backend registration scheme to be list-based (so AutoBlob can be at the end, always) See cle/backends/__init__.py

- Add binwalk as a last-ditch catchall approach. 
Binwalk is really a python library underneath, and my initial exploration showed that this is totally doable. We may want to filter our results to account for the fact that binwalk will generate lots of false-positives, particularly for instruction matching (I bet /dev/urandom produces valid ARM instructions most of the time)
 
How to get started, you ask?
Let me give you a push: https://github.com/angr/cle/tree/feature/autoblob
This has a quick skeleton of the backend itself, with the IVT analysis above implemented as an example. It loads my ARM Cortex M blobs just fine.

Angr folk,

issue_title: GaudiRun returncode in the case of GaudiPython style jobs needs checking
issue_body:
I'll mock up a test job and see if the returncode from running the opts file against Python with:
```python -c myWrappedOptsFile.py```

This should be correct but I'm keen to check there isn't any higher level of return code mangling or interception going on around the users code.

### Version of the SDK being used
```bash
$ pip freeze | grep face
facebook-sdk==2.0.0
```

### Expected Behavior
```python
>>> import facebook
>>> # the next line should not raise an error
>>> facebook.GraphAPI
>>> # yay!
```

### Actual behavior
```python
>>> import facebook
>>> # the next line should not raise an error
>>> facebook.GraphAPI
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: module 'facebook' has no attribute 'GraphAPI'
```

### Steps to Reproduce
This *only* happens in Python 3.6. Watch this:

```bash
$ workon my-35-virtualenv
(my-35-virtualenv) $ python --version
3.5.2
(my-35-virtualenv) $ python
>>> import facebook
>>> facebook.GraphAPI
>>> # yay!
>>> exit(0)
(my-35-virtualenv) $ workon my-36-virtualenv
(my-36-virtualenv) $ python --version
3.6.1
(my-36-virtualenv) $ python
>>> import facebook
>>> facebook.GraphAPI
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: module 'facebook' has no attribute 'GraphAPI'
```

This leads me to believe that python 3.6 changed how imports work. I'll do some research on 3.6 imports, but I wanted this issue to be known. From what I saw there aren't any issues here nor on the facebook developer forum that address this.

In `trpo_mpi.py`, we have the code:

```
def fisher_vector_product(p):
    return allmean(compute_fvp(p, *fvpargs)) + cg_damping * p
```

I'm probably missing something really obvious -- is the `allmean` needed here? The only lines which invoke this function are the `stepdir` and `shs` computations. In the following lines, we have:

```
stepdir = cg(fisher_vector_product, g, cg_iters=cg_iters, verbose=rank==0)
shs = .5*stepdir.dot(fisher_vector_product(stepdir))
```

The quantity `stepdir` appears to depends on values that are guaranteed to be identical across all threads (other than `verbose`); in particular, `g` is guaranteed to be the same across all workers, as `g = allmean(g)` before the conjugate gradient code. This means that `shs` is the same as well across all workers.

Indeed, replacing `fisher_vector_product(p)` by the following seems to work fine:

```
def fisher_vector_product(p):
    a = allmean(compute_fvp(p, *fvpargs)) + cg_damping * p
    b = compute_fvp(p, *fvpargs) + cg_damping * p
    assert np.allclose(a, b)
    return a
```

issue_title: Suggested Helper Methods for package Tools
issue_body:
Here's the Bincrafters `build.py` template.  Some of these are generic enough to be considered for native package tools feature.  Some are not.  Please have a look, feedback welcome: 

```
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

from conan.packager import ConanMultiPackager
import os
import re
import platform

def get_value_from_recipe(search_string):
    with open("conanfile.py", "r") as conanfile:
        contents = conanfile.read()
        result = re.search(search_string, contents)
    return result

def get_name_from_recipe():
    return get_value_from_recipe(r'''name\s*=\s*["'](\S*)["']''').groups()[0]

def get_version_from_recipe():
    return get_value_from_recipe(r'''version\s*=\s*["'](\S*)["']''').groups()[0]

def get_default_vars():
    username = os.getenv("CONAN_USERNAME", "bincrafters")
    channel = os.getenv("CONAN_CHANNEL", "testing")
    version = get_version_from_recipe()
    return username, channel, version

def is_ci_running():
    return os.getenv("APPVEYOR_REPO_NAME", "") or os.getenv("TRAVIS_REPO_SLUG", "")

def get_ci_vars():
    reponame_a = os.getenv("APPVEYOR_REPO_NAME","")
    repobranch_a = os.getenv("APPVEYOR_REPO_BRANCH","")

    reponame_t = os.getenv("TRAVIS_REPO_SLUG","")
    repobranch_t = os.getenv("TRAVIS_BRANCH","")

    username, _ = reponame_a.split("/") if reponame_a else reponame_t.split("/")
    channel, version = repobranch_a.split("/") if repobranch_a else repobranch_t.split("/")
    return username, channel, version

def get_env_vars():
    return get_ci_vars() if is_ci_running() else get_default_vars()

def get_os():
    return platform.system().replace("Darwin", "Macos")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    name = get_name_from_recipe()
    username, channel, version = get_env_vars()
    reference = "{0}/{1}".format(name, version)
    upload = "https://api.bintray.com/conan/{0}/public-conan".format(username)

    builder = ConanMultiPackager(
        username=username,
        channel=channel,
        reference=reference,
        upload=upload,
        remotes=upload,  # while redundant, this moves bincrafters remote to position 0
        upload_only_when_stable=True,
        stable_branch_pattern="stable/*")

    builder.add_common_builds(shared_option_name=name + ":shared")
    builder.run()
```

issue_title: AWS API call retries with exponential backoff
issue_body:
Cfn-Sphere should retry AWS API calls with exponential retry if f.e. a ThrottleError occurs.

http://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/api-retries.html

The implementation of the cli parameter "--parameters" done in https://github.com/cfn-sphere/cfn-sphere/pull/50 uses the following syntax: `my-stack:my-parameter=foo` but the syntax for stack referencing in stack-configuration is `my-stack.my-output`. I think that both should use equal syntax.

This problem has existed on and off for quite some time now - it's also a very temperamental problem - meaning - it goes away, then comes back on its own.

Short version: During commissioning, the joining device gives up with an "Unknown Neighbor" message
the work around seems to be (a) reconfigure the network (new pan id, new passwords) and it seems to fix it self. or wait a little bit and it fixes it self.

Attached are two logs - one is called the NCP.TXT - it represents the debug log from the NCP
The second is joiner.TXT - it represents an MTP device 

What I think is happening is this:  

the precursor - There is or was an earlier failed JOIN operation - for some reason the joiner to be entered into the neighbor table as a valid entry.

The setup - "factory reset" the joining device - now try to rejoin that same device.

In my mind, if and when a device sends a Discovery Request - that should automatically clean up the neighbor table - removing that device from the nieghbor table

====
How did I get this? I enabled the DEBUG UART, and DISABLED the MBED logging ( that generates too much noise)
[ncp.txt](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/files/1742030/ncp.txt)
[joiner.txt](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/files/1742031/joiner.txt)

I'm wondering why in the paper you linked "[Enriching Word Vectors with Subword Information](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1607.04606.pdf)" they compute the similarity between words as the cosine-similarity of the embedded vectors.
The cosine-similarity is also used in most of the projects people have developed using the vectors you shared with us.

The cosine-similarity has been applied very successfully to the term vector space, where the scalar of a vector give as more information on the lenght of the orginal document, instead on its context.

But if the space is shaped by a NN just to discriminate the context given a word, also the scalar of the vectors help the NN to learn the best representation and then generate its output.

Example:
The NN can put a set of related words around the origin of the embedded space, just to map this small weights to a context.
While the cosine similarity between this words can be very high, their euclidean distance will be small.

Do you have any benchmark on this topic that justifies your decision to not take into account the scalar of the vectors, and so to use the cosine similarity instead of the euclidean distance?

Thanks

issue_title: Fix not working Python 3.6 and 3.7 travis enviroment
Behavior of remote targets on local host.
issue_body:
Just to clarify ( #475 ), `remote` targets currently work like this:

1. A `remote` target always `exist()` (returns `true`) locally.
2. A `remote` target, like other non-file targets, are not translated during task preparation.
3. `remote(target)` are replaced with `target` at the beginning of `execute_task`.

It is then clear that `remote` targets only works properly inside tasks. It is not possible to do something like 

```
input: remote('*.txt'), group_by=1
```
although

```
input: remote('file.txt'), remote('file2.txt'), group_by=1
```
works.

You also cannot do

```
output: remote('a.txt'):
sh:
    touch ${output}  
```
since `remote('a.txt')` is not a proper filename, although

```
output: remote('a.txt'):
task:
sh:
    touch ${output}  
```
works all right.

I am not sure if there is any case we should handle `remote` target locally.

issue_title: First field can't start with comment prefix even if quoted
issue_body:
Reading the source code, it looks like checking for comment prefixes is done *after* the line has been parsed and un-quoted as a CSV line. This means that a line is interpreted as comment even if the comment prefix is quoted as part of the first field value of the line, which is probably not extended.

(This does not look easy to fix, unfortunately.)

issue_title: version in setup.py, 1.0.1,  is incorrect for the 1.0.2 tag
issue_body:
The [version recorded in the setup.py file for the 1.0.2 tag](https://github.com/keras-team/keras-preprocessing/blob/1.0.2/setup.py#L24) is 1.0.1, which is incorrect.  The sdist on PyPI has the correct version in the setup.py file.  

This can causes issues if a checkout of the git tag is used as the mean to install keras-preprocessing as the incorrect version will be recorded in the metadata.

I do not think anything should be done to fix this as changing a tag is a bad procedure.  I think it is useful to have this in the issue tracker in case anyone else runs into the issue.

import rdflib

I have released a rdfextras 1.0 - if you re-run easy_install you should be ok!

Add parameters initNs and initBindigs in rdflib.graph.query()

def query(..., initNs={}, initBindings={})

return result(processor.query(query_object, initBindings, initNs))

This should all work fine with rdflib3.1 and new rdfextras - please start a new ticket if you still have problems.
